topics.title: Case Studies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET635 - ENTERPRISE RESOURCE PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Case Studies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of ERP Case Studies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1 – Case Studies)**
> An **ERP Case Study** is a structured, evidence-based analytical document that investigates a real-world organization's decision to adopt, customize, implement, or migrate an Enterprise Resource Planning system. It documents the *business problem*, the *vendor selection process*, the *implementation methodology*, the *measurable outcomes (ROI/TCO)*, and the *critical success factors (CSF)* or *failure causes*.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned), Case Studies are not abstract corporate anecdotes. They are **pedagogical instruments** used to map textbook ERP concepts (like Business Process Reengineering, ASAP methodology, OLAP, MRP-II integration) to **operational reality**. When a student studies an ERP case study, they are essentially reverse-engineering the **Plan → Design → Build → Test → Deploy → Support** lifecycle using a real company as the laboratory.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / The "Hospital Renovation" Intuition

Imagine an old, multi-story hospital where every department (Cardiology, Pharmacy, Billing, Emergency) operates with its own independent paper registers, separate software, and isolated workflows. A patient needing a heart transplant has their file physically shuttled between buildings — losing time, creating data mismatches, and risking their life.

> An **ERP Case Study** is the *architectural blueprint and post-occupancy report* of how that hospital was renovated into a single, integrated smart-building.

- The **Case Study Background** = The hospital's pre-renovation chaos (legacy systems, data silos).
- The **Vendor Selection** = Choosing between contractors (SAP, Oracle, Microsoft Dynamics).
- The **Implementation Methodology** = The phased construction plan (ASAP, AIM, Activate).
- The **Modules Implemented** = The newly integrated departments (FI/CO, MM, SD, HR).
- The **Outcomes** = Reduced patient wait time, unified billing, real-time vitals — i.e., **ROI, reduced TCO, process efficiency**.

### 1.2 Why KTU Specifically Emphasizes ERP Case Studies

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme places Case Studies in **Module 1** intentionally. Before students learn configuration or technical architecture, they must first understand **why** organizations invest crores in ERP projects. The cognitive objective is **"Apply" (Bloom's Level 3)** — mapping theoretical ERP pillars to real industry events.

The **measurable industry benchmarks** that every KTU student must internalize from case studies are:

- **Average Large-Scale ERP Project Cost:** **USD 5 to 50 Million+** (varies by license model and modules).
- **Implementation Duration:** **6 months (SMB)** to **5+ years (Global Fortune 500 rollouts)**.
- **Historical Failure Rate:** Approximately **60–70%** of ERP projects fail to meet original objectives (per classic Standish Group / Panorama Consulting reports).
- **Typical ROI Realization Window:** **3 to 5 years** post go-live.

### 1.3 Categories of ERP Case Studies (KTU 2024 Taxonomy)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the PEIET635 examination, every Case Study discussed in Module 1 falls into one of three categories. The examiner expects students to **classify** the case before analyzing it.

**Table 1.1 — Typology of KTU-Mandated ERP Case Studies**

| # | Category | Defining Characteristic | Classic Example Companies | KTU 2024 Cognitive Tag |
|---|----------|------------------------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Success Case Study** | On-time, on-budget; measurable ROI achieved | Toyota, Toyota Motor (SAP), Hershey (initially troubled, recovered), Coca-Cola, Nike (i2) | *Apply / Analyze* |
| 2 | **Failure / Cautionary Case Study** | Budget/time overruns, user resistance, abandoned modules | Hershey Co. (SAP R/3, 1999), FoxMeyer Drugs, Dell (early SAP exit) | *Analyze / Evaluate* |
| 3 | **Migration / Modernization Case Study** | Transition from legacy (Mainframe/MRP-II) to Cloud ERP | Microsoft (internal), Schlumberger, Colgate-Palmolive | *Evaluate* |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

In KTU valuation, a Case Study answer is **not a story narration**. It is a **structured forensic investigation** broken into exactly seven analytical components. The examiner allocates marks to each block. Missing any block invites a partiality penalty of 2–3 marks.

### 2.1 The 7-Pillar Analytical Framework (Mandatory for Any Case Study)

1. **Company Profile & Business Problem:** Industry, revenue, scale, and the *specific pain point* (data silos, Y2K bug, compliance, M&A integration) that triggered the ERP decision.
2. **Business Objectives & Scope:** Quantified goals — "reduce inventory by 20%", "achieve single-vendor view of customer", "enable IFRS reporting".
3. **Vendor & Module Selection:** Why SAP over Oracle? Why was MM, FI/CO, SD, PP, or HR chosen? Justification based on industry fit.
4. **Implementation Methodology:** Phased approach — Big Bang vs. Phased Rollout (e.g., pilot plant first, then global).
5. **Project Governance:** Steering Committee composition, project manager authority, change management strategy, end-user training hours.
6. **Outcomes (Quantitative & Qualitative):** Hard metrics (cycle time reduction, inventory turns, order-to-cash days) and soft metrics (employee satisfaction, decision-making speed).
7. **Critical Success Factors (CSF) & Lessons Learned:** Top management sponsorship, data cleansing discipline, realistic scope, vendor partnership.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Case Study Evaluation Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In an ESE question worth 14 marks, you are **not expected** to solve numerical equations. However, the *concepts* of TCO, ROI, and payback must be explained using these formulas when discussing outcomes. The examiner rewards **metric awareness**.

**Table 2.1 — High-Yield Metrics for ERP Case Study Analysis**

| Metric | Formula / Definition | Engineering Interpretation in Case Study Context | Typical KTU Question Hook |
|--------|---------------------|--------------------------------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** | $\text{TCO} = \text{License} + \text{Hardware} + \text{Implementation} + \text{Training} + \text{Maintenance} + \text{Upgrades}$ | Used in case studies to compare vendor bids (e.g., why Hershey's USD 115M ballooned). | *"Justify the vendor's TCO claim."* |
| **Return on Investment (ROI)** | $\text{ROI} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Net Benefits} - \text{TCO}}{\text{TCO}} \times 100$ | Quantifies whether the ERP actually paid back. | *"Critically evaluate the ROI claim of the case."* |
| **Payback Period** | $\text{Payback} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Initial TCO}}{\text{Annual Net Cash Benefit}}$ | The "year" when the investment breaks even. | *"If TCO = 50M and annual saving = 10M, payback is 5 years."* |
| **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $\text{NPV} \;=\; \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{\text{Benefit}_{t}}{(1+r)^{t}} \;-\; \text{TCO}$ | Used in greenfield (fresh) implementations to discount future savings. | Rare in KTU, but conceptually referenced. |
| **First Pass Yield (FPY) in ERP Testing** | $\text{FPY} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Defect-free test cases}}{\text{Total test cases}} \times 100$ | Indicates go-live readiness. Low FPY = FoxMeyer-style disaster. | *"Why was FPY critical in the case?"* |
| **Process Cycle Efficiency (PCE)** | $\text{PCE} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Value-Added Time}}{\text{Total Cycle Time}} \times 100$ | Compares pre-ERP vs. post-ERP process efficiency. | *"By what % did PCE improve post-ERP?"* |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** A common 14-mark question stem is: *"The case mentions TCO of USD 50M and projected benefits of USD 15M/year for 6 years. Calculate payback period and comment on feasibility."* A student who merely writes the answer without commenting on **qualitative risks** (resistance, scope creep) loses 4–5 marks.

### 2.3 The Real-World Utility of Studying ERP Case Studies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For an engineering student, ERP Case Studies build **systems-thinking** ability. You learn to see the enterprise as a set of interconnected feedback loops, not isolated departments. This is the same skill used in:
> - **Industrial Engineering:** Production planning and shop-floor control.
> - **Mechanical CAD/CAM integration:** PLM modules of ERP.
> - **Civil/Construction:** Project System (PS) module for large infrastructure projects.
> - **Electrical/Power:** PM module integration with SCADA-derived maintenance data.
> 
> The case study trains you to **speak the language of the CFO, the plant manager, and the IT director in one breath**.

### 2.4 The Anatomy of a Classic KTU Case Study (Hershey – SAP R/3, 1999)

This is the most-tested case in ERP curricula globally and is highly likely in KTU 2024 papers.

- **Problem:** Y2K compliance + need for integrated order-to-cash in 9 months.
- **Vendor & Modules:** SAP R/3 (FI, CO, SD, MM, PP, WM).
- **TCO:** Originally budgeted USD 35M → **Ballooned to USD 115M** (a 230% overrun).
- **Outcome:** Go-live delivered on Halloween 1999, but the system could not process Halloween chocolate orders → **USD 100M+ in lost sales** during peak season.
- **Root Cause of Failure:** Inadequate stress testing of the order-to-cash cycle; over-customization; aggressive timeline.
- **Lesson (CSF for students to quote):** *"A Big Bang go-live is exponentially riskier than a phased rollout. End-to-end integration testing is non-negotiable."*

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Solving a Case Study Question in ESE

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 14-mark Case Study question almost always follows a **fixed template**. Below is the **gold-standard answer architecture** that maps to the examiner's key. Use it for any case you are presented.

### 3.1 The 8-Step ESE Answer Framework (with Logic)

When the question says *"Discuss the ERP implementation at XYZ Corp with reference to Module 1 concepts"*, structure your answer as:

**Step 1 — Context Setting (2 marks):**
Identify the industry, scale, and the **trigger event** (Y2K, merger, IPO, supply chain failure). Never start with "XYZ is a company…" Start with the *trigger*, because the trigger reveals the *scope of urgency*.

**Step 2 — Stated Business Objectives (2 marks):**
Quote the company's own words (e.g., "achieve a 360° view of the customer", "reduce working capital by 18%"). This shows the examiner you have **read** the case.

**Step 3 — Vendor Justification (2 marks):**
Explain *why* the chosen vendor (SAP, Oracle, Microsoft) was selected over competitors. Reference industry-specific templates, language support, and global scalability.

**Step 4 — Module Mapping (2 marks):**
Map business functions to ERP modules. Use a **function-to-module table**. This is where the **Apply**-level Bloom's taxonomy is tested.

**Step 5 — Implementation Methodology (2 marks):**
Specify the rollout type (Big Bang, Phased, Parallel, Pilot) and the methodology (ASAP for SAP, AIM for Oracle, Activate for S/4HANA). The examiner is testing whether the student knows the *methodology is not the same as the software*.

**Step 6 — Outcomes (2 marks):**
State both **quantitative** (TCO, ROI, cycle time, inventory turns) and **qualitative** (better compliance, single source of truth, customer satisfaction) outcomes.

**Step 7 — Critical Success Factors (1 mark):**
List 4–5 CSFs: executive sponsorship, data cleansing, end-user training, realistic scope, change champion network.

**Step 8 — Lessons Learned / Conclusion (1 mark):**
End with a **prescriptive takeaway** — "The case demonstrates that ERP success is 30% technology and 70% change management."

### 3.2 Worked Example — A Simulated KTU Case Study Question

> **Question (14 marks):** *"A leading Indian automotive OEM, with 5 plants and 200+ dealers, plans to implement SAP S/4HANA to integrate procurement, production planning, and dealer order management. The project budget is INR 80 Crore, and the board expects break-even by Year 3. Critically analyze this case using the ERP implementation framework."*

### Model Step-by-Step Answer

**Step 1 — Context (2 marks):**
The trigger is **dealer dissatisfaction and fragmented inventory visibility**. With 5 plants and 200+ dealers on legacy ERPs, the OEM faces order-to-cash delays of 14+ days and a finished-goods inventory of 38 days — far above the industry benchmark of 21 days.

**Step 2 — Objectives (2 marks):**

$$
\text{Target Inventory Days}_{post} = 21 \text{ days}
$$

$$
\text{Target Order-to-Cash}_{post} \leq 4 \text{ days}
$$

$$
\text{Target ROI by Year 3} \geq 25\%
$$

**Step 3 — Vendor Justification (2 marks):**
SAP S/4HANA is selected over Oracle Cloud ERP and Microsoft Dynamics 365 because:
- Pre-built **Automotive Industry Solution (IS-Auto)** template.
- Real-time **MRP Live** capability using HANA in-memory DB.
- Strong **dealer management system (DMS)** integration via SD module with variant configuration (LO-VC).

**Step 4 — Module Mapping (2 marks):**

**Table 3.1 — Business Function to SAP S/4HANA Module Mapping**

| Business Function | SAP Module | Sub-Component |
|--------------------|------------|---------------|
| Procurement of raw materials (steel, electronics) | MM (Materials Management) | Sourcing, PO, Goods Receipt |
| Production scheduling (assembly line) | PP (Production Planning) | MRP Live, Shop Floor Control |
| Dealer order booking & dispatch | SD (Sales & Distribution) | Variant Configuration, ATP Check |
| Financial consolidation across plants | FI/CO | Universal Journal, Profit Center Accounting |
| After-sales warranty | PM/CS | Service Notification, Warranty Claim |

**Step 5 — Methodology (2 marks):**
The OEM adopts a **Phased Rollout with Pilot Plant strategy**:
- **Phase 1 (Months 1–6):** Blueprint & design across one pilot plant and 20 pilot dealers.
- **Phase 2 (Months 7–18):** Rollout to remaining 4 plants and all dealers, parallel run for 60 days.
- **Phase 3 (Months 19–24):** Stabilization, optimization, S/4HANA Cloud extension.

> This avoids the **Hershey-style Big Bang disaster** by isolating risk to a single plant first.

**Step 6 — Outcomes (2 marks):**

$$
\text{TCO} = \text{INR } 80 \text{ Cr (license + impl + training)}
$$

$$
\text{Annual Savings} = \text{INR } 30 \text{ Cr (inventory + labor + scrap reduction)}
$$

$$
\text{Payback Period} = \frac{80}{30} = 2.67 \text{ Years} \quad (\text{Within board's Year 3 target})
$$

**Step 7 — Critical Success Factors (1 mark):**
1. **CFO-CEO Joint Sponsorship:** Without C-suite unity, dealer resistance will paralyze Phase 2.
2. **Master Data Governance:** All 5 plants must align on a **single material master**; otherwise, MRP Live will explode.
3. **Dealer Training:** Minimum 16 hours of role-based training per dealer user; otherwise, order-entry errors will spike.

**Step 8 — Lessons (1 mark):**
The case validates the principle that **phased ERP rollouts in manufacturing reduce integration risk by ~60%** compared to Big Bang. The OEM's success is contingent on master data quality, not software choice.

### 3.3 Common KTU Valuation Mistakes to Avoid

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1:** Writing the case as a "story" without structure — loses 4 marks for lack of analytical rigor.
> **Pitfall 2:** Confusing **ASAP** (SAP's methodology) with **AIM** (Oracle's) and **Activate** (S/4HANA's) — the examiner tests this distinction.
> **Pitfall 3:** Not linking outcomes to **measurable metrics** — "improved efficiency" gets 0 marks; "inventory days reduced from 38 to 22" gets full marks.
> **Pitfall 4:** Forgetting **change management** — case studies without it are treated as incomplete.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics — Visualizing ERP Case Studies

### 4.1 Generic ERP Implementation Lifecycle (Universally Tested)

This diagram is the **canonical structure** the examiner expects in any 14-mark case answer. It maps directly to SAP's ASAP / Oracle's AIM / S/4HANA's Activate methodology.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Phase 1: Project Preparation] --> A2[Phase 2: Business Blueprint]
    A2 --> A3[Phase 3: Realization]
    A3 --> A4[Phase 4: Final Preparation]
    A4 --> A5[Phase 5: Go-Live and Support]
    A5 --> A6[Phase 6: Continuous Improvement]
    A6 -.-> A1
    A1 --> K1[Governance: Steering Committee]
    A2 --> K2[Deliverable: To-Be Process Maps]
    A3 --> K3[Deliverable: Configured System + UAT]
    A4 --> K4[Deliverable: End-User Training Completion]
    A5 --> K5[Deliverable: Hypercare Stabilization]
    A6 --> K6[Deliverable: KPI Dashboard and ROI]
    K1 --- K2
    K2 --- K3
    K3 --- K4
    K4 --- K5
    K5 --- K6
```

### 4.2 Anatomy of a Failed vs. Successful ERP Case Study (Comparative Architecture)

This block-level schematic is the most KTU-relevant visual for an "Evaluate" type question. It shows the same Case Study with two divergent outcomes based on governance.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SuccessPath[SUCCESSFUL ERP IMPLEMENTATION PATH]
        S1[Executive Sponsorship Active] --> S2[Realistic Scope and Timeline]
        S2 --> S3[Master Data Cleansing Rigor]
        S3 --> S4[Phased Rollout with Pilot]
        S4 --> S5[End-User Training 16 Plus Hours]
        S5 --> S6[Go-Live with Hypercare 90 Days]
        S6 --> S7[Measured ROI within 3 Years]
    end
    subgraph FailurePath[FAILED ERP IMPLEMENTATION PATH]
        F1[Weak Sponsorship or Absent C-Suite] --> F2[Scope Creep and Gold Plating]
        F2 --> F3[Dirty Master Data in Legacy]
        F3 --> F4[Big Bang Go-Live in Single Shot]
        F4 --> F5[Insufficient Training Under 4 Hours]
        F5 --> F6[Cutover Chaos and Order Failures]
        F6 --> F7[Cost Overrun and Abandonment]
    end
    S7 -.Contrasted With.-> F7
```

### 4.3 Case Study Analysis Decision Tree (For ESE Time Management)

Use this when you are given a **new, unseen** case in the exam hall to determine your answer path in under 2 minutes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[Read Case Study in 2 Minutes] --> B1{Is Vendor Named?}
    B1 -- Yes --> B2[Identify Vendor and Methodology]
    B1 -- No --> B3[Infer from Industry: SAP for Manufacturing, Oracle for Finance, Dynamics for SMB]
    B2 --> B4{Is TCO Stated?}
    B3 --> B4
    B4 -- Yes --> B5[Compute Payback Period and ROI]
    B4 -- No --> B6[Discuss Qualitative Outcomes Only]
    B5 --> B7[Identify Modules from Business Functions]
    B6 --> B7
    B7 --> B8[Conclude with CSFs and Lessons Learned]
    B8 --> B9[Allocate Marks: Context 2, Objectives 2, Vendor 2, Modules 2, Method 2, Outcomes 2, CSFs 1, Lessons 1]
```

### 4.4 Module-to-Business-Function Mapping (The Heart of Every Case Answer)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Finance[Finance and Controlling]
        FI1[General Ledger]
        CO1[Cost Center Accounting]
        CO2[Profitability Analysis]
    end
    subgraph Logistics[Logistics Chain]
        MM1[Procurement]
        PP1[Production Planning]
        WM1[Warehouse Management]
        SD1[Sales and Distribution]
    end
    subgraph HumanCap[Human Capital]
        HR1[Personnel Administration]
        TM1[Time Management]
        PY1[Payroll]
    end
    subgraph Analytics[Analytics and Reporting]
        BW1[Data Warehousing]
        BO1[Dashboards and KPIs]
    end
    Finance <--> Logistics
    Logistics <--> HumanCap
    Finance <--> Analytics
    HumanCap <--> Analytics
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are modeled on **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**: Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) with internal choice. Marks are tagged with cognitive level for examiner's reference.

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**
*Define the term "ERP Case Study". List the four mandatory components that must be present in a well-structured ERP case study for KTU evaluation purposes.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An ERP Case Study is a structured analytical document examining a real organization's adoption, implementation, or migration of an Enterprise Resource Planning system, capturing the business problem, vendor selection, implementation methodology, and measurable outcomes. The four mandatory components are:
1. **Company Profile and Business Problem** — context and trigger.
2. **Vendor and Module Selection** — justified by business fit.
3. **Implementation Methodology and Governance** — phased, big bang, or parallel; with steering committee structure.
4. **Outcomes (Quantitative and Qualitative)** — TCO, ROI, payback, cycle time, inventory turns, and lessons learned.

*(Cognitive Level: Remember / Understand — 1 mark definition + 2 marks listing.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**
*State any three Critical Success Factors (CSFs) for ERP implementation. Why is "Executive Sponsorship" considered the most critical CSF in any ERP case study?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Three CSFs are: (i) **Executive Sponsorship**, (ii) **Master Data Quality and Cleansing**, (iii) **End-User Training and Change Management**.
Executive Sponsorship is the most critical because ERP projects cut across every department (Finance, HR, Production, Sales). Without unified C-suite authority, departmental resistance, scope creep, and budget overruns paralyze the project. The Hershey and FoxMeyer cases both demonstrate that weak sponsorship led to multi-million-dollar losses. *(Cognitive Level: Understand / Apply — 1 mark each CSF + bonus justification.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Modeled)

> **ESE Module 1 — Question Paper 1, Question 1 (Choice-A)**

#### **Question 1A (14 Marks):** Case Study on a Manufacturing ERP Implementation

> *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1]*
> *"Bharat Forge, a large automotive component manufacturer with 12 plants across India, decided in 2018 to implement SAP S/4HANA to replace a 15-year-old legacy ERP. The project was sanctioned at INR 120 Crore with a 30-month timeline. The CFO mandated a phased rollout starting with the Pune flagship plant. Post go-live at the pilot plant, order fulfillment cycle time dropped from 11 days to 4 days, and finished goods inventory reduced by INR 45 Crore within 8 months. However, during the Baramati plant rollout, severe resistance from floor supervisors caused a 4-month delay."*
> *Analyze this case study using the ERP implementation framework, covering: (a) the business problem and objectives, (b) the chosen methodology, modules, and vendor justification, and (c) the outcomes, CSFs, and lessons learned.*

**(a) Business Problem and Objectives (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Context:** The 15-year-old legacy ERP (likely an MRP-II system) lacked real-time analytics, integration with dealer portals, and IFRS-compliant consolidation. With 12 plants, Bharat Forge faced the classic *fragmented master data* problem: each plant maintained its own material codes, vendor codes, and price lists, leading to procurement inefficiencies and audit fatigue.

**Step 2 — Objectives:**
1. Achieve **unified, real-time visibility** across all 12 plants via SAP S/4HANA's in-memory database.
2. Reduce order fulfillment cycle time from 11 days to **4 days** (a 64% improvement).
3. Free up working capital by **INR 45 Crore** in excess finished goods inventory.
4. Achieve **IFRS and GST-compliant** financial consolidation by FY 2020.

**(b) Methodology, Modules, and Vendor Justification (7 marks):**

**Step 3 — Vendor Justification:** SAP S/4HANA is preferred over Oracle Cloud ERP and Microsoft Dynamics 365 because:
- SAP's **Discrete Manufacturing (IS-Discrete)** and **Automotive (IS-Auto)** industry templates.
- **MRP Live** in HANA enables real-time material requirement planning.
- Existing SAP ecosystem knowledge within Bharat Forge's IT team reduces training cost.

**Step 4 — Module Mapping:**

| Business Function | SAP Module |
|-------------------|------------|
| Raw material procurement | MM (Materials Management) |
| Forging and machining production | PP (Production Planning) + ME (Manufacturing Execution) |
| Finished goods dispatch to OEMs | SD (Sales & Distribution) + EWM (Extended Warehouse Management) |
| Multi-plant financial consolidation | FI/CO (with S/4HANA Universal Journal) |
| Plant maintenance for forging dies | PM (Plant Maintenance) |

**Step 5 — Methodology:** Phased rollout with **Pilot-First Strategy**:
- **Pilot Plant (Pune):** Month 1–10.
- **Wave 2 (Baramati, Satara):** Month 11–20.
- **Wave 3 (remaining 9 plants):** Month 21–30.
- Each wave follows **ASAP methodology** (Prepare → Blueprint → Realize → Deploy → Run).

> This directly mitigates the Hershey-style Big Bang risk.

**(c) Outcomes, CSFs, and Lessons Learned (supplemental, part of the 14 marks):**

**Step 6 — Outcomes (Quantitative):**

$$
\text{Order Cycle Reduction} = \frac{11 - 4}{11} \times 100 = 63.6\%
$$

$$
\text{Working Capital Freed} = \text{INR } 45 \text{ Crore in } 8 \text{ months}
$$

**Step 7 — Critical Success Factors:**
1. **CFO's Sponsorship** unified plant-level financial reporting.
2. **Master Data Harmonization** between legacy plants before pilot go-live.
3. **Phased Rollout** isolated risk to one plant.
4. **Change Champion Network** at the Pune plant (which succeeded).

**Step 8 — Lessons Learned from Baramati Delay:**
The 4-month Baramati delay highlights that **technical success ≠ human success**. The case shows that floor supervisors — not top management — are often the real change resistors. Lesson: **invest 20% of project budget in change management and floor-level training**, not just software configuration.

> **[Internal Choice – Question 1B (14 Marks):]** 
> *"A retail chain with 150 outlets implements Oracle NetSuite ERP to replace a fragmented POS and inventory system. Discuss: (a) the unique retail-specific challenges in module selection, (b) implementation methodology suitable for a multi-outlet retail rollout, and (c) the KPIs that the CFO should track post go-live to demonstrate ROI to the board."* — *Same 7-mark + 7-mark split. Emphasize SD/MM/EPM, phased by region, and KPIs like Same-Store Sales Growth, Gross Margin Return on Inventory (GMROI), and Stockout Rate.*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **MOST COMMON MARK-LOSS ZONES in Module 1 Case Study Questions:**
> 1. **Writing a "story" without numbers:** "They implemented SAP and improved efficiency" = 0 marks. Use TCO, ROI, cycle time, inventory days.
> 2. **Confusing ASAP (SAP) with AIM (Oracle) and Activate (S/4HANA):** Examiner deducts 1–2 marks if methodology is misattributed.
> 3. **Ignoring Change Management:** A 14-mark answer that mentions only software without training, resistance, or sponsorship is treated as **incomplete** — 3-mark penalty.
> 4. **Forgetting to "Critically Analyze":** If the question says *critically analyze*, the examiner expects **both** success factors **and** failure causes. A one-sided "everything was perfect" answer caps at 9/14.
> 5. **No Conclusion / Takeaway:** A 14-mark answer must end with a **prescriptive lesson** applicable to other organizations. Missing this loses 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1 Case Studies**
> - ☐ **Definition:** ERP Case Study = structured forensic analysis of a real-world ERP project covering problem → solution → outcome → lesson.
> - ☐ **Three Categories:** Success, Failure, Migration/Modernization.
> - ☐ **Seven Pillars of Analysis:** Company Profile → Objectives → Vendor/Module → Methodology → Governance → Outcomes → CSFs/Lessons.
> - ☐ **Four Classic KTU-Tested Cases:** Hershey (failure), FoxMeyer (failure), Toyota/Nike (success), Microsoft (migration).
> - ☐ **Three Methodologies to Distinguish:** ASAP (classic SAP), AIM (Oracle), Activate (S/4HANA).
> - ☐ **Key Metrics:** TCO, ROI, Payback Period, NPV, First Pass Yield, Process Cycle Efficiency.
> - ☐ **Big Bang vs. Phased:** Big Bang = single cutover, high risk; Phased = pilot → wave → global, low risk.
> - ☐ **CSFs to Quote:** Executive Sponsorship, Master Data Quality, End-User Training, Realistic Scope, Change Champion Network.
> - ☐ **Mark Allocation Heuristic:** 2+2+2+2+2+2+1+1 = 14 marks across the 8-step framework.
> - ☐ **Golden Sentence to End Any Case Answer:** *"ERP success is 30% technology and 70% change management."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of ERP Package
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET635 - ENTERPRISE RESOURCE PLANNING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Selection of ERP Package # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **ERP Package Selection** is the structured, multi-criteria decision-making (MCDM) process by which an organization identifies, evaluates, shortlists, negotiates, and finally procures the most suitable Enterprise Resource Planning software solution that aligns with its strategic objectives, operational workflows, budget constraints, and long-term scalability requirements. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, ERP package selection is treated as a **strategic procurement activity** that sits at the intersection of business process re-engineering (BPR), vendor management, and IT governance. It is **not** merely a software purchase — it is a *marriage of technology, process, and people* lasting typically **5 to 10 years** for any mid-to-large enterprise. ### Key Terminology Anchored to KTU Syllabus | Term | Definition | |---|---| | **RFI** (Request for Information) | Preliminary document sent to vendors to gather general capability information | | **RFP** (Request for Proposal) | Detailed document specifying organizational requirements sent to shortlisted vendors | | **TCO** (Total Cost of Ownership) | All costs (license, implementation, training, maintenance, upgrades) over the system lifecycle | | **ROI** (Return on Investment) | Financial benefit derived from the ERP system relative to its cost | | **Fit-Gap Analysis** | Mapping of business requirements against standard ERP package functionality | | **Shortlisting** | Reducing the vendor pool (typically 30+ to 3–5) based on weighted criteria | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wedding Selection" Metaphor > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of selecting an ERP package like selecting a *life partner* through an arranged marriage process in a traditional family. **Mapping the Analogy to ERP Selection:** | Real-World Wedding Selection | ERP Package Selection | |---|---| | Family creates a "wish list" of qualities | Organization defines **business requirements** | | Initial screening of 30+ prospects | **RFI** sent to 30+ vendors | | Shortlist of 5 serious candidates | **Shortlisting** to top 5 vendors | | Background checks, meetings, family visits | **RFP, vendor demos, site visits, reference checks** | | Horoscope matching with weightage on finance, family, character | **Fit-Gap Analysis** with **Weighted Scoring Model** | | Final negotiation on dowry, wedding expenses | **Contract negotiation** on license, support, SLAs | | Marriage & settling in (5–10 year commitment) | **Implementation & partnership** (5–10 year commitment) | Just as a hasty marriage leads to regret, a hasty ERP selection leads to **costly project failures** — Gartner's classic statistic states that **more than 55% of ERP projects fail to deliver expected benefits**, and the root cause is often poor package selection. ## 1.3 Why ERP Package Selection is a Critical Decision > [!IMPORTANT] > An ERP system becomes the **digital nervous system** of an organization. Once implemented, switching costs are prohibitive — making the *selection phase* the single most consequential decision in the entire ERP lifecycle. **Critical Reasons:** - **High Financial Risk:** Average ERP implementation costs range from **₹5 Crore to ₹500+ Crore** depending on scale - **Long Lifecycle:** 7–10 years average use before replacement - **Process Lock-in:** Re-engineering business processes to fit software - **Vendor Lock-in:** Proprietary codebases and switching barriers - **Strategic Impact:** Affects every department and every employee --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Six-Phase ERP Package Selection Model The KTU 2024 syllabus prescribes a structured, phase-gate approach. Each phase has deliverables, gate criteria, and stakeholder responsibilities. ### Phase 1: Requirements Definition & Prioritization **Objective:** Translate business strategy into a structured, measurable requirements catalogue. **Key Steps:** 1. Form a **Selection Committee** (CIO, CFO, Functional Heads, End-users) 2. Conduct **Process Discovery Workshops** with each department 3. Document **"MUST-have"** vs **"NICE-to-have"** requirements using the **MoSCoW method** (Must, Should, Could, Won't) 4. Classify requirements into: - **Functional Requirements** (what the system does) - **Technical Requirements** (platform, database, integration) - **Strategic Requirements** (scalability, future-readiness) - **Regulatory Requirements** (compliance, audit trails) > [!NOTE] > **MoSCoW Method** — KTU 2024 High-Yield Topic: > - **M**ust have: System fails without this (e.g., GST module for an Indian company) > - **S**hould have: Important but workaround exists > - **C**ould have: Desirable if budget permits > - **W**on't have (this time): Explicitly out-of-scope ### Phase 2: Market Scanning & Long-Listing **Objective:** Identify the universe of viable ERP vendors. **Vendor Categories to Evaluate:** | Category | Examples | Typical Clientele | |---|---|---| | **Tier 1 — Big 4 / Global Leaders** | SAP S/4HANA, Oracle Cloud ERP, Microsoft Dynamics 365 | Large enterprises, MNCs | | **Tier 2 — Mid-Market Global** | Infor, Epicor, Sage, IFS | Mid-sized firms | | **Tier 3 — Best-of-Breed** | Salesforce, Workday, ServiceNow | Function-specific leaders | | **Tier 4 — Indian/Regional** | Tally, Busy Accounting, Marg, ERPnext, Ramco | SMBs, regional players | | **Tier 5 — Open Source** | Odoo, ERPNext, Apache OFBiz | Cost-sensitive, custom-heavy | ### Phase 3: Long-List to Short-List (Preliminary Filtering) Reduce 30+ vendors to **5–7 finalists** using **knockout criteria**: **Common Knockout Criteria:** - **Industry expertise** — Does vendor serve your industry vertical? - **Financial stability** — Can vendor survive 10 years? - **Local presence** — Implementation partner in Kerala/India? - **Technology stack** — Cloud-native vs on-premise compatibility - **Customer base** — Peer references in similar scale ### Phase 4: Detailed Evaluation (RFP & Weighted Scoring) This is the **mathematical heart** of selection. Vendors are scored on weighted criteria. **Weighted Scoring Model Formula:** $$\text{Vendor Score} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (W_i \times S_i)$$ Where: - $W_i$ = Weight of criterion $i$ (in percentage, $\sum W_i = 100$) - $S_i$ = Score of vendor on criterion $i$ (typically on a 1–5 or 1–10 scale) - $n$ = Total number of evaluation criteria **Why This Formula Matters:** It converts qualitative judgments (vendor demo "felt good") into **quantitative, defensible rankings** that survive board-level scrutiny. ### Phase 5: Vendor Demonstrations & Site Visits - Vendors present **scripted scenarios** based on your actual business processes - Visit **3 reference customers** of each finalist vendor - Assess **change management capability** of vendor - Evaluate **cultural fit** with your team ### Phase 6: Final Selection, Negotiation & Contract - Top 2 vendors receive **Best and Final Offer (BAFO)** request - Negotiate **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** — not just license fee - Finalize **Service Level Agreements (SLAs)** - Sign **Statement of Work (SOW)** and **Master Service Agreement (MSA)** ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Formulas for KTU University Exams** — Memorize these! | # | Formula / Concept | Notation | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Weighted Score** | $\text{Score} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (W_i \times S_i)$ | Rank vendors quantitatively | | 2 | **TCO Calculation** | $\text{TCO} = C_L + C_I + C_T + C_M + C_U + C_D$ | Lifecycle cost analysis | | 3 | **ROI Calculation** | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{(\text{Benefit} - \text{Cost})}{\text{Cost}} \times 100\%$ | Justify ERP investment | | 4 | **Payback Period** | $\text{PP} = \dfrac{\text{Total Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}}$ | Time-to-recover cost | | 5 | **NPV** | $\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - I_0$ | Long-term value of ERP | | 6 | **Discount Factor** | $\text{DF}_t = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^t}$ | Time-value of money | | 7 | **Fit-Gap Ratio** | $\text{Fit \%} = \dfrac{\text{Requirements Met by Standard}}{\text{Total Requirements}} \times 100$ | Standardization potential | | 8 | **Vendor Risk Index** | $\text{VRI} = w_1 F + w_2 S + w_3 T + w_4 R$ | Composite vendor health | | 9 | **Break-Even** | $\text{BE} = \dfrac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{\text{Price} - \text{Variable Cost}}$ | Threshold analysis | ### TCO Component Definitions (High-Yield for KTU) | Symbol | Component | Typical % of TCO | |---|---|---| | $C_L$ | **License Cost** (software) | 15–25% | | $C_I$ | **Implementation Cost** (consulting) | 25–35% | | $C_T$ | **Training Cost** | 8–12% | | $C_M$ | **Maintenance & Support** | 15–20% | | $C_U$ | **Upgrade Cost** | 5–10% | | $C_D$ | **Decommission / Risk Cost** | 5–10% | > [!NOTE] > **Industry Insight:** Most KTU students mistakenly focus on **License Cost** ($C_L$) alone. In reality, $C_L$ is often only **20%** of TCO. The **Implementation cost** ($C_I$) is usually the largest — and this is precisely where selection decisions matter most (cheap software with weak implementation partners is a recipe for disaster). ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility ERP package selection is heavily used in: - **Manufacturing:** Selecting discrete vs process manufacturing modules - **Retail:** Omnichannel inventory management capability - **Healthcare:** HIPAA-compliant patient data modules - **Banking:** Core banking system selection (e.g., Finacle, Temenos) - **Higher Education:** Campus management systems - **Government:** e-Governance ERP deployments --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example: Weighted Scoring Model for 3 Vendors **Problem Context (KTU-style):** A Kerala-based FMCG company shortlisted 3 ERP vendors: **Vendor A (SAP)**, **Vendor B (Oracle)**, **Vendor C (Infor)**. The selection committee assigned weights to 5 criteria and scored each vendor on a 1–10 scale. Calculate the weighted score and rank the vendors. ### Step 1: Define Evaluation Criteria and Weights | Criterion | Weight ($W_i$) | Justification | |---|---|---| | Functional Fit (Industry) | **30%** | Critical for FMCG-specific processes | | Technology Platform (Cloud) | **20%** | Future scalability | | Vendor Financial Health | **20%** | Long-term partnership safety | | Local Implementation Support | **15%** | Kerala-based operations | | Total Cost of Ownership | **15%** | Budget discipline | **Verification of weights:** $$\sum_{i=1}^{5} W_i = 30 + 20 + 20 + 15 + 15 = 100\% \quad \checkmark$$ ### Step 2: Score Each Vendor (1–10 scale) | Criterion | Weight | Vendor A (SAP) | Vendor B (Oracle) | Vendor C (Infor) | |---|---|---|---|---| | Functional Fit | 30 | 9 | 8 | 7 | | Technology Platform | 20 | 8 | 9 | 7 | | Vendor Financial Health | 20 | 10 | 9 | 6 | | Local Implementation Support | 15 | 7 | 6 | 8 | | Total Cost of Ownership | 15 | 5 | 4 | 9 | ### Step 3: Calculate Weighted Score for Vendor A (SAP) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Score}_A &= (W_1 \times S_{1A}) + (W_2 \times S_{2A}) + (W_3 \times S_{3A}) + (W_4 \times S_{4A}) + (W_5 \times S_{5A}) \\ &= (0.30 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 10) + (0.15 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 5) \\ &= 2.70 + 1.60 + 2.00 + 1.05 + 0.75 \\ &= \mathbf{8.10} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4: Calculate Weighted Score for Vendor B (Oracle) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Score}_B &= (0.30 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 6) + (0.15 \times 4) \\ &= 2.40 + 1.80 + 1.80 + 0.90 + 0.60 \\ &= \mathbf{7.50} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5: Calculate Weighted Score for Vendor C (Infor) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Score}_C &= (0.30 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 6) + (0.15 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 9) \\ &= 2.10 + 1.40 + 1.20 + 1.20 + 1.35 \\ &= \mathbf{7.25} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6: Rank the Vendors $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Score}_A > \text{Score}_B > \text{Score}_C \\ 8.10 > 7.50 > 7.25 \end{aligned} $$ **Result: Vendor A (SAP) wins with weighted score 8.10/10.** ## 3.2 Worked Example: ROI Calculation **Problem:** An investment of **₹10 Crore** in ERP is expected to generate annual benefits of **₹2.5 Crore** for 6 years. Calculate the simple ROI. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total Benefit} &= \text{Annual Benefit} \times \text{No. of Years} \\ &= 2.5 \times 6 = 15 \text{ Crore} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Net Benefit} &= \text{Total Benefit} - \text{Total Cost} \\ &= 15 - 10 = 5 \text{ Crore} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{ROI} &= \frac{\text{Net Benefit}}{\text{Total Cost}} \times 100\% \\ &= \frac{5}{10} \times 100\% = \mathbf{50\%} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.3 Worked Example: Payback Period $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Payback Period} &= \frac{\text{Total Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}} \\ &= \frac{10 \text{ Crore}}{2.5 \text{ Crore/year}} = \mathbf{4 \text{ years}} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection Criteria Hierarchy > [!NOTE] > The following **Analytical Decision Matrix** is the single most important KTU-exam tool for this topic. | Tier | Category | Example Criteria | Weight Range | |---|---|---|---| | **Tier 1 (Strategic)** | Business Alignment | Industry fit, Vision match, Innovation roadmap | 30–40% | | **Tier 2 (Operational)** | Functional & Technical | Module coverage, Integration, Customization | 25–35% | | **Tier 3 (Tactical)** | Vendor & Cost | Financial health, TCO, Local support, References | 25–30% | | **Tier 4 (Soft Factors)** | People & Culture | Change management, Training quality, Cultural fit | 10–15% | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern:** When asked to "list criteria for ERP selection," always present them in this **4-tier hierarchy** — examiners award full marks for structured presentation. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Six-Phase ERP Package Selection Process Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Phase 1: Requirements Definition] --> B[Phase 2: Market Scanning and Long-Listing] B --> C[Phase 3: Long-List to Short-List Filtering] C --> D[Phase 4: Detailed RFP and Weighted Scoring] D --> E[Phase 5: Vendor Demos and Site Visits] E --> F[Phase 6: Negotiation and Contract Signing] F --> G[Implementation Phase - Out of Scope] A1[Selection Committee Formation] --> A A2[MoSCoW Prioritization] --> A B1[Identify 30+ Vendors] --> B B2[Categorize Tier 1 to Tier 5] --> B C1[Apply Knockout Criteria] --> C C2[Shortlist 5-7 Vendors] --> C D1[Issue RFP Document] --> D D2[Fit-Gap Analysis] --> D D3[Weighted Scoring Model] --> D E1[Scripted Vendor Demos] --> E E2[Reference Site Visits] --> E F1[Best and Final Offer] --> F F2[SLA and MSA Negotiation] --> F ``` ## 4.2 Weighted Scoring Architecture — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT[Input Layer] A1[Criteria List C1 to Cn] A2[Weights W1 to Wn] A3[Vendor Scores S1 to Sn] end subgraph PROCESS[Processing Engine] B1[Multiply W x S for each criterion] B2[Sum all weighted scores] end subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer] C1[Composite Score per Vendor] C2[Ranked Vendor List] C3[Selection Decision] end A1 --> B1 A2 --> B1 A3 --> B1 B1 --> B2 B2 --> C1 C1 --> C2 C2 --> C3 ``` ## 4.3 RFI to RFP to Contract Funnel ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Market Universe 30+ Vendors] --> RFI[RFI Stage
Eliminate 50 percent] RFI --> Shortlist[Shortlisted 7-10 Vendors] Shortlist --> Knockout[Knockout Criteria Filter] Knockout --> Top5[Top 5 Finalists] Top5 --> RFP[RFP Detailed Evaluation] RFP --> Scoring[Weighted Scoring Model] Scoring --> Demos[Vendor Demonstrations] Demos --> Sites[Reference Site Visits] Sites --> Top2[Top 2 Vendors] Top2 --> BAFO[Best and Final Offer BAFO] BAFO --> Negotiate[Contract Negotiation] Negotiate --> Sign[Master Service Agreement] Sign --> Implement[Implementation Begins] style Start fill:#e1f5ff style Implement fill:#d4edda style Sign fill:#fff3cd ``` ## 4.4 TCO Component Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD TCO[Total Cost of Ownership TCO] --> CL[License Cost 15-25 percent] TCO --> CI[Implementation Cost 25-35 percent] TCO --> CT[Training Cost 8-12 percent] TCO --> CM[Maintenance and Support 15-20 percent] TCO --> CU[Upgrade and Innovation 5-10 percent] TCO --> CD[Risk and Decommission 5-10 percent] CL --> CL1[Perpetual vs Subscription] CL --> CL2[Named vs Concurrent Users] CI --> CI1[Consulting Fees] CI --> CI2[Customization Cost] CI --> CI3[Data Migration] CT --> CT1[End User Training] CT --> CT2[Train the Trainer] CM --> CM1[Annual Support Fee] CM --> CM2[Patch Updates] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]** ### Question 1: Define ERP Package Selection. List any four criteria used in evaluating ERP packages. **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** ERP package selection is the systematic process of identifying, evaluating, and choosing the most suitable enterprise software solution that best fits an organization's business requirements, budget, and strategic objectives. It involves a structured evaluation of available ERP vendors and their offerings to ensure long-term alignment with organizational goals. **Four Criteria (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):** 1. **Functional Fit** — Alignment with business processes 2. **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** — Lifecycle cost analysis 3. **Vendor Financial Stability** — Long-term viability 4. **Technology Platform** — Cloud-native, scalability, integration ### Question 2: What is the MoSCoW method? How is it used in ERP package selection? **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** MoSCoW is a prioritization technique used to classify requirements into four categories: - **M**ust have — Critical, system fails without these - **S**hould have — Important, but workarounds exist - **C**ould have — Desirable, only if budget allows - **W**on't have (this time) — Explicitly out-of-scope for current implementation **Application in ERP Selection (1.5 marks):** During requirements gathering, the selection committee uses MoSCoW to distinguish between *core* functional requirements (e.g., GST compliance for an Indian FMCG company — a "Must") and *nice-to-have* features (e.g., AI-driven demand forecasting — a "Could"). This ensures that the RFP sent to vendors focuses on essentials and avoids over-specification, leading to realistic cost estimates and vendor responses. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice > **[KTU End Semester Exam Pattern - Dec 2024 Style]** ### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1 **Q: Explain in detail the different phases involved in the selection of an ERP package. Discuss the weighted scoring model with a suitable example. (14 marks)** **Model Solution Structure:** #### Part (a): Phases of ERP Package Selection (7 marks) The ERP package selection process consists of **six structured phases**, each with specific deliverables: **Phase 1: Requirements Definition and Prioritization (1.5 marks)** - Form a cross-functional selection committee - Conduct process discovery workshops - Document functional, technical, strategic, and regulatory requirements - Apply **MoSCoW prioritization** to classify requirements **Phase 2: Market Scanning and Long-Listing (1 mark)** - Identify 30+ vendors across Tier 1 to Tier 5 - Industry research and analyst reports (Gartner Magic Quadrant) - Build a long-list categorized by industry vertical and scale **Phase 3: Preliminary Filtering using Knockout Criteria (1 mark)** - Apply non-negotiable filters: industry expertise, financial stability, local presence - Reduce long-list to 5–7 finalists **Phase 4: Detailed Evaluation through RFP and Weighted Scoring (1.5 marks)** - Issue detailed RFP - Conduct **Fit-Gap Analysis** (standard package vs custom requirements) - Apply **Weighted Scoring Model** to rank vendors **Phase 5: Vendor Demonstrations and Reference Site Visits (1 mark)** - Scripted demonstrations based on actual business scenarios - Visit 2–3 reference customers per finalist **Phase 6: Negotiation and Contract Finalization (1 mark)** - Request **Best and Final Offer (BAFO)** - Negotiate TCO, not just license fee - Sign Master Service Agreement (MSA) and Statement of Work (SOW) #### Part (b): Weighted Scoring Model with Example (7 marks) **Conceptual Explanation (2 marks):** The Weighted Scoring Model converts qualitative vendor assessments into quantitative rankings. Each evaluation criterion is assigned: - A **weight** ($W_i$) reflecting its strategic importance (in %) - A **score** ($S_i$) on a 1–10 scale based on vendor capability The composite score is calculated as: $$\text{Vendor Score} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (W_i \times S_i)$$ **Worked Example (5 marks):** A manufacturing company evaluates 3 ERP vendors using 4 criteria: | Criterion | Weight | Vendor X | Vendor Y | Vendor Z | |---|---|---|---|---| | Functional Fit | 40% | 8 | 7 | 9 | | Technology | 25% | 7 | 9 | 6 | | Cost (TCO) | 20% | 6 | 7 | 8 | | Local Support | 15% | 9 | 6 | 7 | **Vendor X Calculation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Score}_X &= (0.40 \times 8) + (0.25 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 6) + (0.15 \times 9) \\ &= 3.20 + 1.75 + 1.20 + 1.35 = \mathbf{7.50} \end{aligned} $$ **Vendor Y Calculation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Score}_Y &= (0.40 \times 7) + (0.25 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 6) \\ &= 2.80 + 2.25 + 1.40 + 0.90 = \mathbf{7.35} \end{aligned} $$ **Vendor Z Calculation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Score}_Z &= (0.40 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 6) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 7) \\ &= 3.60 + 1.50 + 1.60 + 1.05 = \mathbf{7.75} \end{aligned} $$ **Ranking:** Vendor Z (7.75) > Vendor X (7.50) > Vendor Y (7.35) **Conclusion:** Vendor Z is selected despite higher cost, because the high weight on functional fit (40%) — its strongest criterion — drives the overall score. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2 **Q: Discuss the various criteria used in the selection of an ERP package. Explain the Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) model with its components. (14 marks)** **Model Solution Structure:** #### Part (a): Criteria for ERP Package Selection (7 marks) ERP selection criteria are organized into a **four-tier hierarchy:** **Tier 1 — Strategic Criteria (2 marks):** - Alignment with business vision and long-term strategy - Industry-specific functionality and best-practice processes - Vendor's product roadmap and innovation capability - Scalability to support future growth (5–10 years) **Tier 2 — Functional and Technical Criteria (2 marks):** - Coverage of required modules (Finance, HR, Supply Chain, Manufacturing, CRM) - Integration capability with existing systems (APIs, middleware) - Technology stack (Web-based, Cloud-native, Mobile-ready) - Database compatibility and reporting tools - Customization vs configuration flexibility **Tier 3 — Vendor and Cost Criteria (1.5 marks):** - Vendor's financial health and market reputation - Local implementation partner presence and track record - Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) over system lifecycle - Customer references in similar industry - Post-implementation support quality **Tier 4 — People and Cultural Criteria (1.5 marks):** - User-friendliness and learning curve - Quality of training programs offered - Vendor's change management methodology - Cultural fit between vendor team and client team - Availability of trained manpower in market #### Part (b): TCO Model and Components (7 marks) **Conceptual Definition (2 marks):** **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** represents the *complete, lifecycle cost* of owning and operating an ERP system over its useful life (typically 7–10 years). It includes all direct and indirect costs — not just the initial license fee. TCO is a critical selection criterion because it reveals the **true cost** of an ERP investment and enables **apples-to-apples comparison** between vendors offering different pricing models (perpetual license vs subscription). **Mathematical Formula (1 mark):** $$\text{TCO} = C_L + C_I + C_T + C_M + C_U + C_D$$ **Detailed Components (4 marks):** | Component | Symbol | Description | Typical % | |---|---|---|---| | **License Cost** | $C_L$ | One-time perpetual license OR recurring subscription fee. May be per-user, per-module, or enterprise-wide. | 15–25% | | **Implementation Cost** | $C_I$ | Consulting fees for configuration, customization, data migration, and go-live support. Usually the **largest** component. | 25–35% | | **Training Cost** | $C_T$ | End-user training, super-user development, train-the-trainer programs. | 8–12% | | **Maintenance and Support** | $C_M$ | Annual support fees (typically 18–22% of license cost), patches, and helpdesk. | 15–20% | | **Upgrade Cost** | $C_U$ | Future version upgrades, new module rollouts, and innovation adoption. | 5–10% | | **Decommission and Risk Cost** | $C_D$ | Cost of potential data archival, vendor transition, and project failure risk. | 5–10% | **Illustration (built into the 4 marks):** For a **₹20 Crore** ERP project over 8 years: $$ \begin{aligned} C_L &= 20 \times 0.20 = 4 \text{ Crore} \\ C_I &= 20 \times 0.30 = 6 \text{ Crore} \\ C_T &= 20 \times 0.10 = 2 \text{ Crore} \\ C_M &= 20 \times 0.18 \times 8 = 28.8 \text{ Crore (over 8 years)} \\ \text{TCO} &\approx 40.8 \text{ Crore (over 8 years)} \end{aligned} $$ This shows that the **TCO is roughly 2× the initial project cost** when maintenance is included over the lifecycle — a critical insight for KTU exam answers. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Conflating Selection with Implementation** — Selection ends at contract signing; implementation is a separate phase. Do not mix content from Module 3. > 2. **Ignoring TCO** — Students often write only about license cost. Always emphasize TCO with its 6 components. > 3. **No Mathematical Working in Scoring Model** — When asked to "explain weighted scoring model," always show a **numerical example** with calculation. Pure theory = 3–4 marks max; theory + example = full 7 marks. > 4. **Forgetting MoSCoW** — This is a KTU-favorite 2-mark question. Memorize the acronym and apply it. > 5. **Treating Selection as One-Person Decision** — Always mention the **Selection Committee** with cross-functional representation. > 6. **Skipping Knockout Criteria** — The shortlisting phase is incomplete without explaining how 30+ vendors become 5. > 7. **Not Mentioning Reference Site Visits** — A common omission; examiners specifically look for vendor validation techniques. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read 5 minutes before the exam:** - ✅ ERP Package Selection is a **6-phase structured process** (Requirements → Market Scan → Shortlist → Detailed Evaluation → Demos → Negotiation) - ✅ **MoSCoW Method**: Must, Should, Could, Won't — for requirement prioritization - ✅ **RFI** (initial broad info) vs **RFP** (detailed requirements) — know the difference - ✅ **Weighted Scoring Model Formula:** $\text{Score} = \sum (W_i \times S_i)$ - ✅ **TCO Formula:** $\text{TCO} = C_L + C_I + C_T + C_M + C_U + C_D$ (License + Implementation + Training + Maintenance + Upgrade + Decommission) - ✅ **TCO Insight:** License is only **~20%** of total cost — implementation is the largest component - ✅ **ROI Formula:** $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{(\text{Benefit} - \text{Cost})}{\text{Cost}} \times 100\%$ - ✅ **Payback Period:** $\text{PP} = \dfrac{\text{Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}}$ - ✅ **4-Tier Criteria Hierarchy:** Strategic → Functional/Technical → Vendor/Cost → People/Cultural - ✅ **Selection Committee** must include CIO, CFO, Functional Heads, and End-users - ✅ **Knockout Criteria** reduce 30+ vendors to 5; **Weighted Scoring** selects 1 - ✅ **Fit-Gap Analysis** measures how much customization is needed — high fit = lower cost - ✅ **Vendor Tiers:** Tier 1 (SAP, Oracle) → Tier 5 (Open Source like Odoo, ERPNext) - ✅ **Reference Site Visits** are non-negotiable — always validate with peer customers - ✅ **BAFO** (Best and Final Offer) is the standard negotiation trigger with top 2 vendors - ✅ **SLA** (Service Level Agreement) and **MSA** (Master Service Agreement) are the binding documents - ✅ Gartner statistic to remember: **>55% of ERP projects fail to deliver expected benefits** — root cause is poor selection - ✅ Typical ERP lifecycle: **7–10 years**; selection commitment is therefore long-term ---

topics.title: Various Approaches to ERP Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET635 - ENTERPRISE RESOURCE PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Various Approaches to ERP Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ERP Selection** is the strategic, multi-criteria decision-making process by which an organization identifies, evaluates, and chooses the most suitable Enterprise Resource Planning software solution from available alternatives, based on alignment with business requirements, Total Cost of Ownership (TCO), technical architecture, vendor capability, and long-term strategic fit.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEIET635 – Enterprise Resource Planning)**, Module 2 explicitly emphasizes that ERP selection is **not a single-vendor purchase** but a **strategic alignment exercise** between organizational goals and software capabilities. The various approaches to ERP selection define the *methodology* by which this alignment is achieved.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Building a House"**
> Imagine you need to build a house. You have several options:
> 1. **Buy a ready-made villa (Off-the-shelf ERP)** — fast, cheaper, but you cannot change the design much.
> 2. **Hire an architect to design from scratch (Custom-Built ERP)** — perfectly tailored, but very expensive and slow.
> 3. **Buy a kit home and customize rooms (Modular ERP)** — middle ground.
> 4. **Rent a furnished apartment (Cloud/SaaS ERP)** — low upfront cost, monthly rent, less control.
> 5. **Use a community-built plan (Open-Source ERP)** — free blueprint, but you handle repairs yourself.

Each of these represents an **Approach to ERP Selection**. Choosing the right one depends on budget, time, business complexity, and strategic goals — exactly like choosing the right house-building strategy.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test whether students can **differentiate between approaches** (Big Bang vs. Phased, Best-of-Breed vs. Single-Vendor, Custom vs. Package) and **justify the selection** of a particular approach for a given business scenario. This is a **high-weightage Module 2 topic** appearing regularly in Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions.

## 1.4 Core Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Standard Definition |
|---|---|
| **TCO (Total Cost of Ownership)** | The complete lifecycle cost of an ERP system over its operational lifetime. |
| **ROI (Return on Investment)** | Financial metric measuring the efficiency of an ERP investment. |
| **Big Bang Approach** | Implementing the entire ERP system across the organization in a single cutover. |
| **Phased/Modular Approach** | Implementing ERP modules sequentially, one department/function at a time. |
| **Best-of-Breed** | Selecting specialized point solutions from multiple vendors for best functionality. |
| **Single-Vendor Suite** | Selecting all modules from one ERP vendor for tight integration. |
| **SaaS (Software as a Service)** | Cloud-hosted ERP delivered via subscription model. |
| **RFP (Request for Proposal)** | Formal document soliciting vendor proposals based on requirements. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of ERP Selection Approaches

ERP selection approaches can be broadly classified into **four strategic dimensions**:

### Dimension 1: By **Source of Software**
1. **Off-the-Shelf / Package ERP** (e.g., SAP, Oracle, Microsoft Dynamics)
2. **Custom-Built ERP** (developed in-house or by a third party from scratch)
3. **Open-Source ERP** (e.g., Odoo, ERPNext, Compiere)
4. **Cloud/SaaS ERP** (e.g., NetSuite, SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Oracle Fusion Cloud)

### Dimension 2: By **Vendor Strategy**
1. **Single-Vendor Suite Approach** — One vendor, all modules
2. **Best-of-Breed Approach** — Multiple vendors, best-in-class modules
3. **Hybrid Approach** — Core single-vendor + specialized best-of-breed add-ons

### Dimension 3: By **Implementation Rollout**
1. **Big Bang Approach** — Full organization at once
2. **Phased / Modular Approach** — Department-by-department rollout
3. **Pilot Approach** — Single site, then scale
4. **Parallel Approach** — Old and new systems run simultaneously

### Dimension 4: By **Customization Depth**
1. **Vanilla Approach** — Use the ERP as-is, change business to fit software
2. **Customization-Heavy Approach** — Modify ERP to match business processes
3. **Configuration Approach** — Use built-in settings (middle path)

## 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Each Major Approach

### A. The Big Bang Approach

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition**: The Big Bang approach involves implementing the **entire ERP system across all departments, functions, and locations simultaneously** at a single "go-live" date.

**Why choose Big Bang?**
- Eliminates dual-system operation costs
- Faster realization of ROI
- Forces organizational-wide change management
- Avoids complex multi-phase integration

**Why avoid Big Bang?**
- **Extremely high risk** — no fallback if it fails
- Resource-intensive (everyone trained at once)
- Difficult to manage change resistance
- Single failure point affects the entire organization

### B. The Phased / Modular Approach

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition**: The Phased approach implements ERP in **sequential stages**, typically starting with core modules (Finance, HR) and gradually adding others (SCM, CRM, Manufacturing).

**Why choose Phased?**
- Lower risk per phase
- Lessons learned from Phase 1 improve later phases
- Easier change management (smaller user groups)
- Quicker initial ROI from high-priority modules

**Why avoid Phased?**
- Longer total implementation timeline
- Integration complexity between phases
- "Project fatigue" among employees
- Two systems may run in parallel for extended periods

### C. Best-of-Breed Approach

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition**: The Best-of-Breed approach selects **the best available software in each functional area** from potentially different vendors.

**Why choose Best-of-Breed?**
- Superior functionality in each module
- Competitive pricing through vendor competition
- Flexibility to replace any single module

**Why avoid Best-of-Breed?**
- **Integration nightmare** — different vendors, different architectures
- Higher Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)
- Multiple vendor relationships to manage
- Inconsistent user experience

### D. Single-Vendor Suite Approach

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition**: The Single-Vendor Suite approach selects **all ERP modules from one vendor**, ensuring native integration.

**Why choose Single-Vendor Suite?**
- Seamless integration (designed to work together)
- Single point of accountability
- Lower long-term TCO
- Consistent UI and database

**Why avoid Single-Vendor Suite?**
- May have weak modules (forced compromise)
- Vendor lock-in (switching cost is high)
- Limited negotiating leverage after contract

### E. Custom-Built ERP

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition**: A Custom-Built ERP is developed **specifically for one organization**, either in-house or by a contracted developer team.

| Parameter | Custom-Built |
|---|---|
| **Cost** | Very High (₹50 lakh – ₹100+ crore) |
| **Time** | 2–5 years |
| **Fit** | Perfect for unique processes |
| **Risk** | Very High (developer dependency) |
| **Maintenance** | Difficult without original developers |

### F. Cloud / SaaS ERP

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition**: Cloud ERP is hosted on the vendor's servers and accessed via the internet, typically on a **subscription (per-user-per-month)** basis.

**Advantages**: Low upfront CAPEX, fast deployment, automatic updates, scalability.
**Disadvantages**: Data security concerns, ongoing OPEX, limited customization, vendor lock-in.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (TCO & ROI)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **directly tested** in KTU 2024 Scheme numerical questions on ERP justification.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $TCO = C_{HW} + C_{SW} + C_{Impl} + C_{Train} + C_{Maint} + C_{Down}$ | Hardware, Software, Implementation, Training, Maintenance, Downtime costs | ₹ (INR) |
| 2 | $ROI = \dfrac{Net\ Benefit}{Total\ Cost} \times 100$ | Net Benefit = (Gains − Costs) | Percentage (%) |
| 3 | $Payback\ Period = \dfrac{Initial\ Investment}{Annual\ Cash\ Inflow}$ | Time to recover investment | Years |
| 4 | $NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - I_0$ | $CF_t$ = Cash flow at time t, $r$ = discount rate, $I_0$ = initial investment | ₹ (INR) |
| 5 | $Break\ Even\ Point = \dfrac{Fixed\ Cost}{Selling\ Price - Variable\ Cost\ per\ unit}$ | Minimum units to cover costs | Units |
| 6 | $Cost\ per\ User = \dfrac{Total\ TCO}{Number\ of\ Users \times Useful\ Life\ (years)}$ | Average cost burden per user | ₹ / user / year |
| 7 | $Customization\ Ratio = \dfrac{Number\ of\ Modified\ Modules}{Total\ Number\ of\ Modules} \times 100$ | Measures degree of deviation from vanilla | Percentage (%) |
| 8 | $Risk\ Score = \sum (P_i \times I_i)$ | $P_i$ = Probability of risk $i$, $I_i$ = Impact of risk $i$ | Score (1–25) |

> **Where:**
> - $C_{HW}$ = Hardware Cost
> - $C_{SW}$ = Software License Cost
> - $C_{Impl}$ = Implementation & Consulting Cost
> - $C_{Train}$ = Training Cost
> - $C_{Maint}$ = Annual Maintenance Cost
> - $C_{Down}$ = Downtime / Failure Cost

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In actual industrial practice, the **choice of ERP selection approach** determines:
- **Manufacturing firms** often prefer **Modular Phased Rollout** to avoid production halts.
- **SMEs (Small & Medium Enterprises)** increasingly adopt **Cloud SaaS ERP** to minimize CAPEX.
- **Government / Public Sector** typically mandates **Open-Source ERP** to avoid vendor lock-in.
- **Multinational Corporations** prefer **Single-Vendor Global Suite** (e.g., SAP S/4HANA) for cross-border standardization.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Comparison Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Comparison Matrix: All ERP Selection Approaches

| Comparison Parameter | Big Bang | Phased | Best-of-Breed | Single-Suite | Custom-Built | Cloud SaaS | Open-Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Implementation Time** | Short (6–12 mo) | Long (2–4 yr) | Medium | Medium | Very Long (3–5 yr) | Very Short (1–3 mo) | Short-Medium |
| **Total Cost** | High | Medium-High | High | Medium | Very High | Low-Mid OPEX | Low |
| **Integration Risk** | Low (single event) | Medium | Very High | Very Low | N/A (custom) | Low | Medium |
| **Business Disruption** | Very High | Low-Medium | Medium | Medium | High | Very Low | Low |
| **Customization** | Limited | Limited | High | Limited | Unlimited | Limited | High |
| **Vendor Lock-in** | High | High | Low | Very High | None (own code) | Very High | None |
| **Best Suited For** | Small orgs, urgency | Large orgs, high risk | Specialized industries | Large multi-nationals | Unique processes | SMEs, fast growth | Cost-sensitive, tech-savvy |
| **KTU Frequency** | Very High | Very High | High | Medium | Medium | High | Medium |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Numerical Derivation: TCO Calculation (14-Mark Type)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Style Numerical Problem**: A manufacturing company in Kerala evaluates two ERP options. Calculate the **5-year TCO** for each and determine which is financially superior.

### Given Data

**Option A — SAP S/4HANA On-Premise (Single-Suite):**
- Hardware Cost = ₹80,00,000
- Software License = ₹1,20,00,000
- Implementation & Consulting = ₹50,00,000
- Training (one-time) = ₹15,00,000
- Annual Maintenance = ₹20,00,000/year
- Annual Downtime Cost = ₹5,00,000/year

**Option B — Oracle NetSuite Cloud (SaaS):**
- Setup Cost = ₹10,00,000
- Subscription Fee = ₹30,00,000/year
- Training (one-time) = ₹8,00,000
- Annual Maintenance = ₹2,00,000/year
- Annual Downtime Cost = ₹8,00,000/year

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Calculate 5-year TCO for Option A (On-Premise SAP)**

TCO consists of **one-time costs** + **recurring costs over 5 years**.

$$TCO_A = C_{HW} + C_{SW} + C_{Impl} + C_{Train} + (C_{Maint} \times 5) + (C_{Down} \times 5)$$

Substitute the values from Option A:

$$TCO_A = 80{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}00{,}000 + 15{,}00{,}000 + (20{,}00{,}000 \times 5) + (5{,}00{,}000 \times 5)$$

**Step 2: Compute each term for Option A**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{One-time Costs} &= 80{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}00{,}000 + 15{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 2{,}65{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR} \\
\text{Recurring (5 years)} &= (20{,}00{,}000 \times 5) + (5{,}00{,}000 \times 5) \\
&= 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 + 25{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 1{,}25{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Total TCO for Option A**

$$TCO_A = 2{,}65{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}25{,}00{,}000 = 3{,}90{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}$$

**Step 4: Calculate 5-year TCO for Option B (Cloud SaaS)**

$$TCO_B = C_{Setup} + C_{Train} + (C_{Sub} \times 5) + (C_{Maint} \times 5) + (C_{Down} \times 5)$$

Substitute the values:

$$TCO_B = 10{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}00{,}000 + (30{,}00{,}000 \times 5) + (2{,}00{,}000 \times 5) + (8{,}00{,}000 \times 5)$$

**Step 5: Compute each term for Option B**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{One-time Costs} &= 10{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}00{,}000 = 18{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR} \\
\text{Recurring (5 years)} &= (30{,}00{,}000 \times 5) + (2{,}00{,}000 \times 5) + (8{,}00{,}000 \times 5) \\
&= 1{,}50{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000 + 40{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Total TCO for Option B**

$$TCO_B = 18{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 = 2{,}18{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}$$

**Step 7: Decision Rule**

$$\Delta TCO = TCO_A - TCO_B = 3{,}90{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}18{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}72{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}$$

**Step 8: Calculate Cost per User per Year (Assume 100 users, 5-year life)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost per User per Year (Option A)} &= \dfrac{3{,}90{,}00{,}000}{100 \times 5} = \dfrac{3{,}90{,}00{,}000}{500} = 78{,}000 \text{ INR/user/year} \\
\text{Cost per User per Year (Option B)} &= \dfrac{2{,}18{,}00{,}000}{100 \times 5} = \dfrac{2{,}18{,}00{,}000}{500} = 43{,}600 \text{ INR/user/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9: Final Conclusion**

> **Option B (Cloud SaaS – Oracle NetSuite) is financially superior**, saving **₹1,72,00,000** over 5 years and reducing per-user cost by **₹34,400/year** (a **44% reduction**).

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: ROI Calculation

Suppose the company in the previous example expects **annual operational savings of ₹80,00,000** from process automation and faster reporting.

**Step 1: Calculate Net Annual Benefit**

For **Option A** (On-Premise):
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Annual Benefit}_A &= \text{Savings} - \text{Annual Maintenance} - \text{Downtime} \\
&= 80{,}00{,}000 - 20{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 55{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

For **Option B** (Cloud SaaS):
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Annual Benefit}_B &= \text{Savings} - \text{Subscription} - \text{Maintenance} - \text{Downtime} \\
&= 80{,}00{,}000 - 30{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 40{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute 5-Year ROI**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ROI_A &= \dfrac{Net\ Benefit_A \times 5 - Initial\ Investment_A}{Initial\ Investment_A} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{(55{,}00{,}000 \times 5) - 2{,}65{,}00{,}000}{2{,}65{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{2{,}75{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}65{,}00{,}000}{2{,}65{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{10{,}00{,}000}{2{,}65{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \approx 3.77\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
ROI_B &= \dfrac{Net\ Benefit_B \times 5 - Initial\ Investment_B}{Initial\ Investment_B} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{(40{,}00{,}000 \times 5) - 18{,}00{,}000}{18{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{2{,}00{,}00{,}000 - 18{,}00{,}000}{18{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{1{,}82{,}00{,}000}{18{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \approx 1011.11\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Interpret the Results**

> **Option B (Cloud SaaS) yields a 5-year ROI of ~1011%**, while **Option A (On-Premise) yields only ~3.77%** — a dramatic difference. **Option B is decisively superior in both TCO and ROI.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams**: Cloud SaaS ERP has a **low CAPEX** but **high recurring OPEX**. The financial crossover point (where on-premise becomes cheaper than cloud) typically occurs between **Years 7 and 10**. For an SME planning a 5-year horizon, SaaS is overwhelmingly cost-effective.

## 3.4 Payback Period Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Payback}_A &= \dfrac{2{,}65{,}00{,}000}{55{,}00{,}000} \approx 4.82 \text{ years} \\
\text{Payback}_B &= \dfrac{18{,}00{,}000}{40{,}00{,}000} = 0.45 \text{ years (about 5.4 months)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Option B recovers its initial investment in just 5.4 months**, while Option A takes nearly 5 years.
