4. **Material Engineering** — Curriculum, sandbox, simulations, job-aids, and SOPs.
5. **Evaluation & Feedback Loop** — Continuous measurement using the **Kirkpatrick-Phillips** framework.

#### B. The Three Phases of ERP Training

| Phase | Timing | Objective | Primary Audience |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Phase 1 — Pre-Implementation (TNA + Core Team Training)** | Project Kick-off → Blueprint | Equip the core implementation team with product knowledge | Functional & Technical Consultants |
| **Phase 2 — End-User Training (Sandbox + Pilot)** | Realization → UAT | Train end-users in the *to-be* processes | End-Users & Super-Users |
| **Phase 3 — Post-Implementation (Refresher + KT)** | Go-Live → Hyper-care | Sustain skills, handle edge-cases, transfer ownership | All Users + IT Support |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Concept:**
> *"Why is training conducted in phases rather than as a single event?"*
> **Answer:** ERP modules go-live sequentially. Users of the **Finance module** cannot be trained on **Production Planning** if the latter's configuration is not yet complete. Phasing ensures **just-in-time competency**.

#### C. Types of ERP Training (Audience-Based Classification)

- **Executive / Management Training:** High-level dashboards, KPIs, strategic decision support.
- **Functional / Process Training:** Detailed workflow training (e.g., Procure-to-Pay, Order-to-Cash).
- **Technical Training:** ABAP, OData services, BAPIs, BADI, integration protocols, Fiori/UI5.
- **End-User Training:** Day-to-day transactional mastery (creating POs, running MRP, posting invoices).
- **Super-User / Champion Training:** Advanced troubleshooting, report generation, peer coaching.
- **Train-the-Trainer (TTT):** A meta-programme where internal trainers are certified to deliver further sessions.

#### D. Training Delivery Methods — A Comparative Matrix

| Method | Description | Advantages | Disadvantages | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Instructor-Led Training (ILT)** | Classroom sessions by certified trainers | Real-time Q&A, hands-on demos | High cost, scheduling issues | Core team & super-users |
| **E-Learning / WBT** | Self-paced online modules (SCORM) | Scalable, cost-effective, 24/7 | Low engagement, no personalization | Geographically dispersed users |
| **Web-Based Training (VLT)** | Live virtual classroom via Zoom/Teams | Combines ILT benefits with remote flexibility | Internet dependency | Hybrid workforces |
| **On-the-Job Training (OJT)** | Learning while performing real tasks in sandbox | Contextual, retention high | Risk of corrupting live data | Go-live stabilization |
| **Blended Learning** | Mix of ILT + e-Learning + OJT | Balanced cost, depth, and reach | Complex logistics | Enterprise-wide rollouts |
| **Sandbox Training** | Practice in a non-production, anonymized environment | Zero risk to live data | Requires dedicated infra | All functional users |

#### E. Kirkpatrick's Four-Level Evaluation Model (The Golden Framework)

This is the **most-tested framework in KTU ERP papers**. Developed by Donald Kirkpatrick (1959), it evaluates training effectiveness at four ascending levels:

| Level | What it Measures | Typical Metric | KTU Question Style |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L1 — Reaction** | Did trainees *like* the programme? | Feedback form score (1–5) | "Define L1 evaluation." |
| **L2 — Learning** | Did trainees *acquire* knowledge/skills? | Pre-test vs Post-test score improvement | "How is L2 measured?" |
| **L3 — Behaviour** | Did trainees *apply* skills on the job? | Supervisor observation, audit | "Differentiate L2 from L3." |
| **L4 — Results** | Did the training *impact business* outcomes? | Error rate reduction, process cycle time, ROI | "Why is L4 the hardest to measure?" |

The **Phillips ROI Methodology** (1996) added a **Level 5 — ROI**, converting business impact into monetary terms.

### 📊 KTU Formula Sheet — ERP Training Analytics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **high-yield** for KTU numerical questions (typically appearing as 3-mark problems in Part A or sub-parts of 14-mark questions).

| # | Metric | Formula | Units | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Training Effectiveness Ratio (TER)** | $\text{TER} = \dfrac{\text{Post-Test Score} - \text{Pre-Test Score}}{\text{Maximum Possible Score}}$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) | Closer to 1 → Higher learning gain |
| 2 | **Training ROI (%)** | $\text{ROI \%} = \dfrac{(\text{Benefits} - \text{Costs})}{\text{Costs}} \times 100$ | Percentage | Benchmark: ROI > 25% is considered excellent |
| 3 | **Cost per Trainee (CPT)** | $\text{CPT} = \dfrac{\text{Total Training Cost}}{\text{Number of Trainees}}$ | Currency / person | Used to budget large rollouts |
| 4 | **Learning Curve Improvement** | $T_n = T_1 \cdot n^{\log_2(\text{learning rate})}$ | Hours | $T_1$ = first-unit time, $n$ = unit number |
| 5 | **User Adoption Rate (UAR)** | $\text{UAR} = \dfrac{\text{Active Users}}{\text{Licensed Seats}} \times 100$ | Percentage | Healthy: UAR > 80% within 90 days post go-live |
| 6 | **Training Coverage Index (TCI)** | $\text{TCI} = \dfrac{\text{Users Trained}}{\text{Total Target Users}} \times 100$ | Percentage | Target: TCI = 100% before go-live |
| 7 | **Knowledge Retention Rate** | $\text{KRR} = \dfrac{\text{Score at } t_{30}}{\text{Score at } t_{0}} \times 100$ | Percentage | Measured 30 days post-training |
| 8 | **Error Rate Reduction (ERR)** | $\text{ERR \%} = \dfrac{E_{\text{before}} - E_{\text{after}}}{E_{\text{before}}} \times 100$ | Percentage | Direct proxy for L4 (Results) |

### 🌍 Real-World Utility of ERP Training

- In **SAP S/4HANA** rollouts, vendors like Accenture & Deloitte mandate **>40 hours of training per functional consultant** and **>16 hours per end-user**.
- In **Tata Steel's ERP migration**, training coverage of **12,000+ employees** over 6 months was the single largest budget line after software licensing.
- In **Kerala State IT Mission's IKM (Information Kerala Mission)** projects, *Vernacular Language Training* (Malayalam-localized SAP screens) became a KTU-style case study on inclusive ERP adoption.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 🔬 Derivative 1 — Kirkpatrick-Phillips ROI Computation (Full Worked Example)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most likely 7-mark numerical** in KTU Part B questions.

**Problem Statement:**
An IT company in Technopark, Trivandrum invested in an ERP training programme. The data is as follows:

- Total training cost (instructor fees + materials + sandbox infra) = **₹ 5,00,000**
- Number of trainees = **50**
- Improvement in productivity (annual monetary benefit) = **₹ 8,00,000**
- Improvement in error reduction (annual monetary benefit) = **₹ 3,00,000**
- Improvement in customer retention (annual monetary benefit) = **₹ 4,00,000**
- Intangible benefits (morale, brand) — *excluded from ROI calculation per Phillips*

**Step 1: Compute the Total Annual Monetary Benefits.**

$$
B_{\text{total}} = B_{\text{productivity}} + B_{\text{error}} + B_{\text{retention}}
$$

$$
B_{\text{total}} = 8{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
B_{\text{total}} = 15{,}00{,}000 \; \text{INR}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the Phillips ROI Formula.**

$$
\text{ROI \%} = \dfrac{B_{\text{total}} - C_{\text{total}}}{C_{\text{total}}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{ROI \%} = \dfrac{15{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{ROI \%} = \dfrac{10{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{ROI \%} = 200 \%
$$

**Step 3: Interpret the result.**

$$
\boxed{\text{ROI} = 200\% \implies \text{For every } \text{₹ } 1 \text{ spent on training, the company recovered } \text{₹ } 2 \text{ as net profit.}}
$$

**Step 4: Derive the Cost per Trainee (CPT) — a complementary KTU metric.**

$$
\text{CPT} = \dfrac{C_{\text{total}}}{N_{\text{trainees}}}
$$

$$
\text{CPT} = \dfrac{5{,}00{,}000}{50}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{CPT} = \text{₹ } 10{,}000 \text{ per trainee}}
$$

---

### 🔬 Derivative 2 — Learning Curve Application in ERP Mastery

The **learning curve** models how an employee's transaction-processing time decreases as they repeat a task.

**Given:**

- Time to process the first Purchase Order ($T_1$) = **40 minutes**
- Learning rate ($LR$) = **80%** (each doubling of volume reduces time by 20%)
- Number of purchase orders processed so far ($n$) = **8**

**Step 1: Apply the Learning Curve Equation.**

$$
T_n = T_1 \cdot n^{b}, \quad \text{where } b = \log_2(LR)
$$

$$
b = \log_2(0.80) = -0.3219
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values.**

$$
T_8 = 40 \cdot 8^{-0.3219}
$$

$$
T_8 = 40 \cdot 0.5
$$

$$
\boxed{T_8 = 20 \text{ minutes}}
$$

**Step 3: Interpret.**

$$
\text{The user's PO-processing time has halved from 40 min to 20 min after 8 repetitions — a hallmark of effective ERP training retention.}
$$

---

### 🔬 Derivative 3 — Training Coverage & Adoption Combined Index

**Given:**

- Total target users = **1,000**
- Users trained = **900**
- Users actively transacting in ERP (90 days post go-live) = **750**
- Licensed seats purchased = **1,200**

**Step 1: Training Coverage Index (TCI).**

$$
\text{TCI} = \dfrac{900}{1{,}000} \times 100 = 90 \%
$$

**Step 2: User Adoption Rate (UAR).**

$$
\text{UAR} = \dfrac{750}{1{,}200} \times 100 = 62.5 \%
$$

**Step 3: Joint Interpretation.**

$$
\boxed{\text{TCI} = 90\% \text{ but } \text{UAR} = 62.5\% \implies \text{Training coverage was good, but } \approx 27.5\% \text{ of trained users are NOT actively using the ERP.}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** A high TCI alone is misleading. The KTU paper setter will expect you to compute **both TCI and UAR** and interpret the *gap* — which signals resistance, poor UX, or inadequate sandbox training.

---

### 🔬 Derivative 4 — Effectiveness Ratio (TER) Pre/Post Test

**Given:**

- Pre-test average score (out of 100) = **35**
- Post-test average score (out of 100) = **78**

$$
\text{TER} = \dfrac{78 - 35}{100} = 0.43
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{TER} = 0.43 \text{ (43\% improvement) — acceptable threshold for ERP end-user training is } \geq 0.40}
$$

---

### 📋 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Training Method

| Audience Size | Geographic Spread | System Complexity | Recommended Method | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| < 50 | Single site | High | ILT + Sandbox | Personal attention critical |
| 50–500 | Multi-site | Medium | Blended (ILT + e-Learning) | Balances cost & depth |
| > 500 | Pan-India | Low | E-Learning + TTT | Scalability dominant |
| < 30 | Single site | Very High (ABAP/Fiori) | ILT only | Mentorship essential |
| 100–200 | Hybrid | Medium | VLT + Sandbox | Remote + hands-on |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 📊 Diagram 1 — End-to-End ERP Training Life-Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Kick-off] --> B[TNA - Training Needs Analysis]
    B --> C{Identify Audience}
    C --> D1[Executives]
    C --> D2[Functional Consultants]
    C --> D3[Technical Consultants]
    C --> D4[End-Users]
    C --> D5[Super-Users]

    D1 --> E1[Management Dashboard Training]
    D2 --> E2[Process & Configuration Training]
    D3 --> E3[Technical ABAP Integration Training]
    D4 --> E4[Transactional Sandbox Training]
    D5 --> E5[Train-the-Trainer TTT]

    E1 --> F[Sandbox Practice Phase]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F

    F --> G[User Acceptance Testing - UAT]
    G --> H[Go-Live Readiness Check]
    H --> I[Go-Live Cut-over]
    I --> J[Hyper-care Support 0-90 Days]
    J --> K[Refresher Training]
    K --> L[Knowledge Transfer KT to Internal IT]
    L --> M[Continuous Improvement Loop]

    M --> B
```

### 📊 Diagram 2 — Kirkpatrick-Phillips Five-Level Evaluation Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L5[L5 ROI - Monetary Conversion] --> L4
    L4[L4 Results - Business Impact] --> L3
    L3[L3 Behaviour - On-the-Job Application] --> L2
    L2[L2 Learning - Knowledge & Skills Acquired] --> L1
    L1[L1 Reaction - Trainee Satisfaction]

    L1 -.- M1[Measured via Feedback Forms]
    L2 -.- M2[Measured via Pre-Post Tests]
    L3 -.- M3[Measured via Supervisor Observation and Audits]
    L4 -.- M4[Measured via KPIs like Cycle Time and Error Rate]
    L5 -.- M5[Measured via Cost-Benefit Analysis]
```

### 📊 Diagram 3 — Functional Block Architecture of an ERP Training System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT
        I1[Legacy Process Maps]
        I2[To-Be Blueprints]
        I3[User Role Matrices]
    end

    subgraph ENGINE
        EN1[TNA Module]
        EN2[Curriculum Designer]
        EN3[Sandbox Provisioning Engine]
        EN4[LMS - Learning Management System]
        EN5[Assessment Engine]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT
        O1[Training Calendar]
        O2[E-Learning Modules]
        O3[Instructor-Led Sessions]
        O4[Job Aids and SOPs]
        O5[Certification Records]
    end

    subgraph FEEDBACK
        F1[Level 1-5 Evaluation Dashboard]
        F2[Trainer Performance Score]
        F3[Learner Progress Reports]
    end

    I1 --> EN1
    I2 --> EN1
    I3 --> EN1

    EN1 --> EN2
    EN2 --> EN3
    EN2 --> EN4
    EN3 --> EN5

    EN3 --> O1
    EN3 --> O3
    EN4 --> O2
    EN2 --> O4
    EN5 --> O5

    O1 --> F1
    O3 --> F2
    O2 --> F3
    O5 --> F3
```

### 📊 Diagram 4 — Audience-Based Training Depth Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TOP[Executive Leadership] --> M1[Strategic KPIs and Dashboards - 8 Hours]
    M1 --> M2[Functional Process Champions - 40 Hours]
    M2 --> M3[Super-Users and Power Users - 60 Hours]
    M3 --> M4[End-Users and Operators - 16-24 Hours]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** The **inverted pyramid** (Diagram 4) reflects the real-world reality — *fewer* users at the top need *less* training in the software itself but *more* training in interpreting dashboards; *more* users at the bottom need *concrete, transactional* training.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define ERP training. Why is it considered the most critical success factor in ERP implementation?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
ERP training is the structured process of imparting system, process, and behavioural competencies to all stakeholders of an ERP project — executives, functional consultants, technical staff, super-users, and end-users — so that they can effectively implement, operate, and continuously improve the system. **(1 mark)**
It is the most critical success factor because: **(2 marks)**
1. Without trained users, even a perfectly configured ERP fails at adoption (>70% of ERP failures are linked to inadequate training).
2. Training bridges the gap between *legacy process comfort* and *new system behaviour*.
3. It converts CAPEX into measurable OPEX savings by enabling correct, first-time transactions and reducing error rates.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List the four levels of Kirkpatrick's training evaluation model with one metric for each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Level | Description | Metric |
|---|---|---|
| L1 — Reaction | Trainee's perception of the programme | Feedback score (1–5 Likert) |
| L2 — Learning | Knowledge/skill acquired | Pre-test vs Post-test improvement |
| L3 — Behaviour | Application on the job | Supervisor observation / audit |
| L4 — Results | Business impact | Error rate reduction / cycle time |

*(1 mark for the table / 4 sub-points, 1 mark for examples, 1 mark for conclusion.)*

---

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### ❓ QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper]**
*(a)* Explain in detail the **three phases of ERP training** with reference to a real-world SAP implementation in a manufacturing company. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Discuss the **Kirkpatrick-Phillips Five-Level Evaluation Model** with suitable examples from ERP end-user training. Compute the **Training ROI** for the following case: **(7 marks)**

> *An FMCG company in Kochi spent ₹ 6,00,000 on training 60 employees. Post-training, productivity gains were valued at ₹ 10,00,000 annually, error reduction savings at ₹ 4,00,000 annually, and customer retention gains at ₹ 3,00,000 annually. Calculate the ROI % and the Cost Per Trainee.*

---

##### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Three Phases of ERP Training (7 Marks):**

**Phase 1 — Pre-Implementation Training (Project Kick-off to Blueprint Stage):** **(2 marks)**
- Audience: Core implementation team, functional consultants, project managers.
- Objective: Build product knowledge and process mapping skills.
- Example: The SAP FI/CO consultants of a Kochi-based spice-export company attend a 5-day intensive course on SAP S/4HANA Finance before blueprinting begins.

**Phase 2 — End-User Training (Realization to User Acceptance Testing):** **(2.5 marks)**
- Audience: End-users and super-users.
- Objective: Train users on the *to-be* processes using a sandbox environment.
- Example: Warehouse operators of the same company practice Goods Receipt, Put-away, and Stock Transfer in a sandbox mirroring production for 3 weeks before UAT.

**Phase 3 — Post-Implementation Training (Go-Live to Hyper-care):** **(2.5 marks)**
- Audience: All users + internal IT support.
- Objective: Sustain skills, handle edge cases, perform knowledge transfer.
- Example: After go-live, refreshers are conducted every fortnight for 3 months; super-users are certified to handle L1/L2 tickets.

---

**Part (b) — Kirkpatrick-Phillips Model + ROI Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Kirkpatrick-Phillips Model Explanation (4 marks):**

| Level | Description | ERP Example |
|---|---|---|
| **L1 — Reaction** | Trainee satisfaction with the course | "How would you rate the SAP MM training?" → 4.5 / 5 |
| **L2 — Learning** | Knowledge & skill gained | Pre-test 30 → Post-test 75 in SAP MM transaction codes |
| **L3 — Behaviour** | On-the-job application | 85% of trained users raise POs in SAP without supervisor help |
| **L4 — Results** | Business impact | PO cycle time reduced from 4 days to 1.5 days |
| **L5 — ROI (Phillips)** | Monetary conversion of L4 | ₹ 17 Lakh benefit vs ₹ 6 Lakh cost → ROI = 183% |

**ROI Calculation (3 marks):**

$$
B_{\text{total}} = 10{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 = 17{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

$$
\text{ROI \%} = \dfrac{B_{\text{total}} - C_{\text{total}}}{C_{\text{total}}} \times 100 = \dfrac{17{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{ROI \%} = \dfrac{11{,}00{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 183.33 \%
$$

$$
\text{CPT} = \dfrac{6{,}00{,}000}{60} = \text{₹ } 10{,}000 \text{ per trainee}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{ROI} = 183.33\%, \quad \text{CPT} = \text{₹ } 10{,}000}
$$

---

#### ❓ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Supplementary Paper]**
*(a)* Discuss the **different types of ERP training** based on audience and the **various delivery methods** used. Compare any two methods with a SWOT-style analysis. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Explain the concept of **Training Needs Analysis (TNA)**. How would you conduct a TNA for an ERP rollout in a 500-employee textile company in Kannur? List at least five data-collection techniques. **(7 marks)**

---

##### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Types & Methods of ERP Training (7 Marks):**

**Types by Audience (3 marks):**
1. **Executive Training** — Strategic, dashboard-focused (e.g., SAP Fiori analytical apps for the MD).
2. **Functional Training** — Process-specific (e.g., FICO, MM, SD, PP consultants).
3. **Technical Training** — ABAP, OData, BAPIs, integration, Fiori/UI5.
4. **End-User Training** — Transactional mastery (PO creation, invoice posting, GRN).
5. **Super-User / TTT Training** — Champions who train their peers post go-live.

**Delivery Methods (2 marks):** ILT, e-Learning, VLT, OJT, Blended, Sandbox.

**SWOT Comparison — ILT vs E-Learning (2 marks):**

| Criterion | ILT | E-Learning |
|---|---|---|
| **Strength** | Real-time Q&A, hands-on demos | Scalable, 24/7 access |
| **Weakness** | High cost, scheduling issues | Low engagement, isolation |
| **Opportunity** | Best for complex topics | Best for geographically dispersed users |
| **Threat** | Trainer availability | Internet / LMS downtime |

---

**Part (b) — TNA for Kannur Textile Company (7 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Training Needs Analysis (TNA) is a systematic process of identifying the *gap* between the **current competencies** of employees and the **future competencies required** to operate the new ERP system effectively.

**TNA Process for the 500-employee Kannur Textile Company (3 marks):**

1. **Organizational Analysis:** Identify company-wide goals (e.g., reduce order-fulfillment cycle from 7 days to 3 days).
2. **Task Analysis:** Identify the new ERP-dependent tasks (e.g., yarn procurement in SAP MM, payroll in SAP HCM, weaving-floor scheduling in SAP PP).
3. **Person Analysis:** Identify the existing skill levels of spinners, weavers, supervisors, accountants, and HR staff.

**Five Data-Collection Techniques (3 marks):**

1. **Questionnaires / Surveys** — Distributed to all 500 employees in Malayalam & English.
2. **Interviews** — One-on-one with department heads.
3. **Focus Groups** — 8–10 weavers per session to discuss daily friction points.
4. **Observation / Job Shadowing** — Watch loom operators perform current manual processes.
5. **Document Analysis** — Review existing SOPs, MIS reports, and legacy system logs.
6. *(Bonus)* **Skills Audit Tests** — A practical test on basic computer literacy.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing TNA with Training Evaluation.** TNA happens *before* training; evaluation happens *after*. Examiners *will* deduct 1–2 marks if you mix them up.
> 2. **Forgetting the "People Dimension."** ERP training is not just software training — it is *change management*. A 14-mark answer that ignores resistance, morale, and culture will lose **at least 3 marks**.
> 3. **Skipping the ROI formula steps.** Always show the full derivation — $B_{\text{total}}$, then plug into the ROI equation. Never jump directly to a percentage.
> 4. **Ignoring the cost of a Sandbox.** If you recommend ILT or sandbox training, you **must** mention the infrastructure cost (LMS licenses, sandbox servers) — KTU valuators reward "holistic thinking."
> 5. **Misnaming the Kirkpatrick levels.** L1 is **Reaction** (not "Response"), and L3 is **Behaviour** (not "Application") — these are KTU-textbook-exact terms.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — ERP Training**

- ✅ ERP Training is a **structured, multi-phased** knowledge-transfer programme — *not* a one-time classroom event.
- ✅ It is the **single largest CSF** in the People Dimension of ERP success.
- ✅ **Three Phases:** Pre-Implementation → End-User → Post-Implementation (Refresher + KT).
- ✅ **Audience Hierarchy (inverted pyramid):** Executives < Functional < Technical < Super-Users < End-Users (largest in number).
- ✅ **Delivery Methods:** ILT, E-Learning, VLT, OJT, Blended, Sandbox — each suited to a different audience size and complexity.
- ✅ **Kirkpatrick's 4 Levels:** Reaction → Learning → Behaviour → Results.
- ✅ **Phillips' 5th Level:** ROI — converts L4 into monetary terms.
- ✅ **TNA (Training Needs Analysis)** is the prerequisite step — *never skip it*.
- ✅ **Super-Users / Power Users** are the unsung heroes of go-live stabilization.
- ✅ **Sandbox Environment** is mandatory — training on live production data is malpractice.
- ✅ **Vernacular / Local Language** training (e.g., Malayalam UI) boosts adoption in Kerala-based rollouts.
- ✅ **ROI > 25%** is considered a *good* ERP training outcome; **< 0%** indicates training failure.
- ✅ **Common KTU buzzwords:** *Change Management, Knowledge Transfer, Just-in-Time Training, Adoption Gap, Training Coverage Index.*
- ✅ **Mnemonic for Kirkpatrick:** **R-L-B-R** → **R**eaction, **L**earning, **B**ehaviour, **R**esults (or **"Really Lovable Brilliant Results"** 😄).
- ✅ **Mnemonic for TNA steps:** **OTP** → **O**rganizational, **T**ask, **P**erson Analysis.

> **🎓 Final Exam Mantra:**
> *"In every ERP answer, dedicate at least 2 lines to the **HUMAN side** — resistance, morale, culture, and language. KTU rewards the holistic thinker who remembers that ERP is ultimately a **People Project** powered by **Software**."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Change Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET635 - ENTERPRISE RESOURCE PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Change Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Change Management (CM)** in the context of Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) is defined as the structured, systematic, and holistic approach to transitioning individuals, teams, and the entire organization from a current operational state to a desired future state, wherein the future state involves the adoption, integration, and sustained use of a new ERP system and its associated business processes.

According to the **Prosci ADKAR Methodology** (a global benchmark adopted in ERP roll-outs), Change Management is the application of a structured process and set of tools for leading the *people side* of change to achieve a desired outcome. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, CM is treated as a **critical success factor (CSF)** that addresses the *human, procedural, and cultural* dimensions of ERP implementation — distinct from the technical configuration of the software itself.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Terminology to Memorize:**
> - **People side of change** — refers to the human/cultural dimension of ERP adoption.
> - **Current State vs. Future State** — the "as-is" versus "to-be" operational models.
> - **Change Curve** — the emotional and productivity trajectory employees follow during transition.
> - **Sustainment** — the final phase where the new ERP behaviors become the organizational norm.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a 100-year-old family restaurant in Kerala that has always served food on banana leaves, with recipes memorized by heart and billing done on a manual ledger. Suddenly, the owner decides to install a **fully automated POS (Point of Sale) system, online ordering, and digital inventory tracking**.

The software can be installed in **3 days** by the IT vendor. But will the senior cooks, waiters, and cashiers — many of whom have never touched a computer — start using it effectively the next morning? Almost certainly **not**.

This gap between **technical installation** and **operational adoption** is precisely what **Change Management** addresses. It is the invisible, human-centered discipline that ensures people:
1. **Understand** *why* the new ERP is necessary,
2. **Are trained and equipped** to use it,
3. **Desire** to support it, and
4. **Continue** using it long after the consultants have left.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Statistic (widely cited in ERP literature):** Approximately **70% of ERP project failures** are attributed to poor change management and lack of user adoption — not to software defects. This is a high-yield line for KTU 14-mark answers.

### 1.3 Why Change Management is the Hardest Part of ERP

ERP systems force **process standardization**, **role redefinition**, **reporting hierarchy shifts**, and **cultural transparency** (because data is no longer hidden in silos). These are deep organizational changes disguised as a software project. KTU examiners expect students to recognize that:

- The **technical scope** of an ERP project covers ~**20%** of the work.
- The **change management scope** covers the remaining **~80%** — people, processes, and culture.

This 80/20 insight is a **favorite 3-mark question** in KTU exams.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Dimensions of ERP Change

Every ERP-driven change simultaneously impacts three tightly coupled dimensions:

1. **Technological Dimension** — new hardware, software, network, and database architecture.
2. **Process Dimension** — redesigned, standardized, and automated business workflows.
3. **People / Cultural Dimension** — new roles, new skills, altered power structures, and a shift toward data-driven decision-making.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** A common student mistake is to treat CM as a *training program*. It is **far broader** — it includes communication planning, sponsorship, resistance management, cultural alignment, and post-go-live reinforcement.

### 2.2 Classical Change Management Models (Mandatory for KTU)

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects familiarity with the **three foundational models** below.

#### 2.2.1 Lewin's 3-Stage Change Model (Kurt Lewin, 1947)

This is the **simplest and most frequently tested** model.

| Stage | Name | ERP Context Activity |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Unfreeze** | Communicate the need for ERP, break the status quo, build urgency, address resistance. |
| 2 | **Change (Transition)** | Train users, pilot the system, redesign processes, deploy modules in waves. |
| 3 | **Refreeze** | Lock in new behaviors, celebrate wins, update KPIs, embed ERP into organizational DNA. |

#### 2.2.2 Kotter's 8-Step Change Model (John Kotter, Harvard)

A sequential, more granular roadmap that builds on Lewin's model.

1. **Create a Sense of Urgency** — executive communication about competitive pressure.
2. **Build a Guiding Coalition** — appoint executive sponsor + change champion network.
3. **Form a Strategic Vision** — define the *to-be* state and link ERP to business value.
4. **Enlist a Volunteer Army** — recruit change agents across departments.
5. **Enable Action by Removing Barriers** — dismantle old approval hierarchies that block new ERP workflows.
6. **Generate Short-Term Wins** — celebrate early pilot successes (e.g., a successful Finance module go-live).
7. **Sustain Acceleration** — keep momentum after initial wins; avoid declaring victory too early.
8. **Institute Change (Anchor in Culture)** — embed ERP usage into HR policies, performance reviews, and SOPs.

#### 2.2.3 ADKAR Model (Prosci)

A **goal-oriented** model focused on the *individual* moving through change. Each letter is a milestone the person must achieve:

- **A — Awareness** of the need for change.
- **D — Desire** to support and participate in the change.
- **K — Knowledge** on how to change (skills and training).
- **A — Ability** to implement the change (hands-on competence).
- **R — Reinforcement** to sustain the change (recognition, rewards, audits).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trick Question Pattern:** "If an employee is *aware* of the ERP rollout and *desires* to use it but has not been trained, which ADKAR element is missing?"
> **Answer:** **Knowledge** — and consequently, **Ability** will also be missing.

### 2.3 The Change Management Process in an ERP Lifecycle

The end-to-end process can be mapped into the ERP project phases:

1. **Preparation Phase** — Stakeholder analysis, change impact assessment, sponsorship activation.
2. **Planning Phase** — Communication plan, training plan, resistance management plan.
3. **Implementation Phase** — Conduct training, deploy change champions, manage resistance in real-time.
4. **Post-Implementation (Sustainment) Phase** — Measure adoption KPIs, celebrate success, continuously improve.

### 2.4 Resistance to Change — Causes and Mitigation

Resistance is the **natural human response** to perceived loss — of competence, status, routine, or control.

| Cause of Resistance | ERP-Specific Example | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| Fear of job loss | Automation of manual ledger entries | Transparent communication on role evolution; reskilling |
| Loss of status / power | Department head losing control of "their" data | Involve them early as process owners / champions |
| Poor communication | Rumors about layoffs due to ERP | Multi-channel, honest, two-way communication |
| Lack of training | Users revert to old manual workarounds | Role-based, hands-on training + floor-walking support |
| Bad past experience | Previous failed IT project | Visible executive sponsorship + quick wins |
| Incompatible culture | "We have always done it this way" | Cultural alignment workshops; celebrate early adopters |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "F.L.O.W.E.R.S."** for causes of resistance:
> **F**ear, **L**oss of control, **O**ld habits, **W**orkload increase, **E**go/status, **R**umors, **S**kepticism of management.

### 2.5 The Role of Executive Sponsor and Change Champion Network

- **Executive Sponsor:** A C-level leader (e.g., CFO) who *owns* the change, removes organizational roadblocks, and models the new behaviors. Without a credible sponsor, ERP projects lose political legitimacy.
- **Change Champion Network:** A cross-departmental team of influential employees who advocate for the ERP, gather frontline feedback, and act as peer-coaches. They are the **bridge** between the project team and end-users.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Framework / Concept | Originator | Core Strength | ERP Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 3-Stage Model | Kurt Lewin | Simplicity, foundational theory | High-level ERP roll-out phases |
| 8-Step Model | John Kotter | Action-oriented, sequential | End-to-end ERP transformation program |
| ADKAR | Prosci (Hiatt) | Individual-focused, measurable | Per-user adoption tracking and gap analysis |
| Resistance Diagnosis (F.L.O.W.E.R.S.) | Academic synthesis | Symptom-to-cause mapping | Pre-go-live readiness assessment |
| Sponsor-Champion-User Triad | Prosci & Best Practice | Role clarity in CM | Governance structure for ERP CM team |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern **Industry 4.0 / Smart Manufacturing** contexts, change management extends to enabling shop-floor workers to trust and use **IoT dashboards, MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems), and AI-driven predictive maintenance tools**. The same ADKAR logic applies — awareness, desire, knowledge, ability, and reinforcement determine whether a technology investment actually delivers ROI on the factory floor.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework: Applying CM to an ERP Case

> **Note:** Change Management is a behavioral and management discipline; therefore, the "derivations" in this module are *analytical frameworks* — step-by-step structured reasoning processes rather than mathematical proofs. Every step is explicitly justified, mirroring the rigor expected in a KTU 14-mark answer.

### 3.1 Case Study (Typical KTU 14-Mark Question Format)

> *A mid-sized manufacturing company in Ernakulam has decided to implement SAP S/4HANA. The CFO is the executive sponsor. The project is 6 months from go-live. The shop-floor supervisors have openly stated that the new system is "useless" and they will continue to maintain paper records. Apply a structured Change Management plan to address this situation.*

### 3.2 Analytical Solution (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Diagnose the Current State and Identify the Change Type**

$$
\text{Change Type} = f(\text{Process}, \text{Technology}, \text{People}, \text{Culture})
$$

In this case, the change is **transformational** (not incremental) because SAP S/4HANA will alter reporting hierarchies, automate manual work, and introduce real-time analytics — all of which are perceived as threatening by the shop-floor supervisors.

**Step 2 — Apply ADKAR Gap Analysis to the Shop-Floor Supervisors**

| ADKAR Element | Current State | Gap Identified |
|---|---|---|
| **A**wareness | They know the project exists but do not see its personal benefit | **Yes** — gap in *personal* relevance |
| **D**esire | Openly hostile — "useless" remark | **Major gap** — desire is negative |
| **K**nowledge | No hands-on exposure yet | **Yes** — training gap |
| **A**bility | Currently zero — never used SAP | **Yes** — competency gap |
| **R**einforcement | No KPIs tied to system use | **Yes** — no incentive structure |

**Conclusion of Step 2:** The *biggest* gap is at the **Desire** level. Kotter's model and Prosci research both confirm that **Desire is the hardest element to build** — you cannot train your way out of a desire problem.

**Step 3 — Select the Appropriate CM Model**

For a *desire problem in a specific stakeholder group*, the **ADKAR + Kotter hybrid** is optimal:
- Use **Kotter Steps 1–4** to create organizational pull (urgency, coalition, vision, volunteer army).
- Use **ADKAR** as the per-person diagnostic to target interventions.

**Step 4 — Design Targeted Interventions (using Lewin's Unfreeze → Change → Refreeze logic)**

| Lewin Stage | Targeted Intervention | Owner |
|---|---|---|
| **Unfreeze** | CFO personally walks the shop floor, demonstrates how SAP will reduce their *paperwork burden* and *give them real-time production data they never had before* | Executive Sponsor |
| **Unfreeze** | Recruit 2 respected senior supervisors as **Change Champions** — leverage peer influence | Change Manager |
| **Change** | Role-based, hands-on SAP training on the *actual screens they will use daily* | Training Lead |
| **Change** | Run a **pilot on one production line** for 4 weeks, with floor-walking support 24×7 | Project Manager |
| **Refreeze** | Update supervisor KPIs to include "system data accuracy" as a 10% weightage in their performance review | HR + CFO |
| **Refreeze** | Publicly celebrate the pilot line's on-time delivery improvement in the town-hall meeting | Communications Lead |

**Step 5 — Define Adoption Metrics (the "R" in ADKAR and the "Refreeze" proof)**

$$
\text{Adoption Rate (\%)} = \frac{\text{Number of users actively logging in and transacting in SAP}}{\text{Total number of intended SAP users}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Data Quality Index} = \frac{\text{Number of fields populated correctly}}{\text{Total number of mandatory fields in a process}} \times 100
$$

A typical KTU-suitable **adoption threshold** for declaring project success is:

$$
\text{Adoption Rate} \geq 90\% \quad \text{AND} \quad \text{Data Quality Index} \geq 95\%
$$

**Step 6 — Risk and Sustainment Plan**

- **Risk:** If the CFO is transferred mid-project, sponsorship collapses.
  *Mitigation:* Identify a backup executive sponsor and have them publicly co-own the vision.

- **Risk:** Supervisors revert to paper records 60 days after go-live.
  *Mitigation:* Monthly adoption dashboards reviewed in steering committee; recognize top adopters.

**Step 7 — Continuous Improvement Loop**

$$
\text{Plan} \rightarrow \text{Do} \rightarrow \text{Check (using KPIs)} \rightarrow \text{Act} \rightarrow \text{Plan} \ldots
$$

This is the **PDCA cycle** applied to change sustainment — a high-yield KTU concept.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix Summary

| KTU Module 2 Learning Outcome | Covered by Step |
|---|---|
| Define Change Management and its role in ERP | Step 1 |
| Identify classical CM models | Step 3 |
| Diagnose resistance and apply ADKAR | Step 2 |
| Design role-based CM interventions | Step 4 |
| Measure adoption and sustain change | Steps 5, 6, 7 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Lewin's 3-Stage Change Model (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Current State / Status Quo] --> B[Stage 1: Unfreeze]
    B --> B1[Create Awareness of Need for Change]
    B --> B2[Build Urgency and Sponsorship]
    B --> C[Stage 2: Change / Transition]
    C --> C1[Train Users on New ERP]
    C --> C2[Deploy Pilot Modules]
    C --> C3[Address Resistance in Real Time]
    C --> D[Stage 3: Refreeze]
    D --> D1[Lock in New Behaviors]
    D --> D2[Update KPIs and SOPs]
    D --> D3[Anchor ERP into Culture]
    D --> E[New Desired State]
```

### 4.2 Kotter's 8-Step Change Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Create Sense of Urgency] --> S2[Step 2: Build Guiding Coalition]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Form Strategic Vision]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Enlist a Volunteer Army]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Enable Action Remove Barriers]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Generate Short Term Wins]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Sustain Acceleration]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Institute Change in Culture]
    S8 --> RESULT[ERP Transformed Organization]
```

### 4.3 ADKAR Model — Individual Change Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AW[Awareness of Need] --> DE[Desire to Support]
    DE --> KN[Knowledge on How to Change]
    KN --> AB[Ability to Implement]
    AB --> RE[Reinforcement to Sustain]
    RE --> OUT[Individual Adoption Achieved]
```

### 4.4 ERP Change Management Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PHASE1[Phase 1: Preparation]
        P1A[Stakeholder Analysis]
        P1B[Change Impact Assessment]
        P1C[Activate Executive Sponsor]
    end
    subgraph PHASE2[Phase 2: Planning]
        P2A[Communication Plan]
        P2B[Training Plan]
        P2C[Resistance Management Plan]
    end
    subgraph PHASE3[Phase 3: Implementation]
        P3A[Deliver Training Programs]
        P3B[Deploy Change Champion Network]
        P3C[Run Pilot and Full Go Live]
    end
    subgraph PHASE4[Phase 4: Sustainment]
        P4A[Track Adoption KPIs]
        P4B[Continuous Reinforcement]
        P4C[Iterate and Improve]
    end
    PHASE1 --> PHASE2 --> PHASE3 --> PHASE4
    PHASE4 -.->|Feedback Loop| PHASE2
```

### 4.5 Resistance Management Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Identify Resistance Symptom] --> R2{Is it Awareness Gap?}
    R2 -- Yes --> R2A[Run Town Hall and Targeted Comms]
    R2 -- No --> R3{Is it Desire Gap?}
    R3 -- Yes --> R3A[Engage Sponsor + Peer Champions]
    R3 -- No --> R4{Is it Knowledge or Ability Gap?}
    R4 -- Yes --> R4A[Role Based Hands on Training]
    R4 -- No --> R5{Is it Reinforcement Gap?}
    R5 -- Yes --> R5A[Tie KPIs and Rewards to System Use]
    R5 -- No --> R6[Re diagnose Root Cause via 1 on 1 Interviews]
```

### 4.6 Sponsor — Champion — End User Triad

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Executive Sponsor e.g. CFO] -->|Sets Vision Removes Roadblocks| CH[Change Champion Network]
    CH -->|Coaches and Advocates| EU[End Users Shop Floor Managers]
    EU -->|Feedback and Adoption Data| CH
    CH -->|Escalation and Insights| SP
    SP -->|Visible Behaviors and Communication| EU
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set A]**
*Define Change Management in the context of ERP. Why is it considered the most critical success factor in ERP implementation?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Change Management is the structured approach to transitioning individuals, teams, and organizations from the current state to a desired future state, addressing the people, process, and cultural dimensions of adopting an ERP system.
*(1 mark for definition)*

It is the most critical success factor because approximately **70% of ERP project failures** are attributed to poor user adoption, resistance, and lack of leadership sponsorship — not to software defects. While the technical installation may take weeks, behavioral adoption takes months or years, and without active CM, the full ROI of the ERP investment is never realized.
*(2 marks for justification with the 70% statistic and ROI argument)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*List and briefly explain any three causes of resistance to change during an ERP implementation.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark per cause):**
1. **Fear of job loss:** Employees worry that automation will make their roles redundant, triggering defensive behavior.
2. **Loss of status or control:** Department heads may lose informal power when data transparency reveals previously hidden information.
3. **Lack of training / knowledge gap:** Users revert to old manual workarounds if they are not confident in operating the new system.

*(Accept any valid three causes from the F.L.O.W.E.R.S. mnemonic: Fear, Loss of control, Old habits, Workload increase, Ego, Rumors, Skepticism.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain **Kotter's 8-Step Change Model** in detail. How is it applied to manage change in a large-scale ERP implementation project? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **ADKAR model**. For each element, describe one specific ERP-related intervention a Change Manager would design. *(7 marks)*

---

**Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Kotter's 8-Step Model — Applied to ERP (7 marks)**

The model provides a sequential roadmap for organizational transformation:

1. **Create a Sense of Urgency** *(1 mark)* — The executive sponsor communicates market pressures (e.g., competitor adoption, regulatory compliance deadlines) to mobilize the organization. In ERP, this is typically done through quarterly town halls and steering committee updates.

2. **Build a Guiding Coalition** *(1 mark)* — A cross-functional leadership team (CIO, CFO, HR Head, Plant Head) is formed to co-own the ERP vision and provide unified direction.

3. **Form a Strategic Vision** *(1 mark)* — A clear *to-be* state is articulated: "Become a data-driven enterprise with real-time decision-making by Q4." This vision links ERP to business outcomes, not technology.

4. **Enlist a Volunteer Army** *(0.5 mark)* — Change champions are recruited from every department to act as peer-influencers and feedback conduits.

5. **Enable Action by Removing Barriers** *(1 mark)* — Old approval workflows and manual data silos are dismantled. IT legacy systems that block integration are decommissioned.

6. **Generate Short-Term Wins** *(0.5 mark)* — The Finance module is piloted first; on-time invoice processing improvement is celebrated publicly to build credibility.

7. **Sustain Acceleration** *(1 mark)* — After early wins, additional modules (HR, Procurement) are rolled out; complacency is explicitly guarded against.

8. **Institute Change in Culture** *(1 mark)* — ERP usage is embedded into onboarding, performance reviews, and SOPs. The change becomes "the way we work."

**ERP-Specific Insight (bonus 0.5 mark):** Kotter's model is *top-down* and *organization-wide*; it complements but does not replace ADKAR, which is *bottom-up* and *individual-focused*.

---

**(b) ADKAR Model — Element-Wise ERP Interventions (7 marks = 1.4 marks × 5 elements)**

| ADKAR Element | Definition | ERP-Specific Intervention |
|---|---|---|
| **A — Awareness** | Understanding *why* change is needed | CEO email + video explaining how manual processes caused last year's audit failures; ERP is the fix. |
| **D — Desire** | Personal willingness to support and engage | Involve employees early as process owners; offer certification bonuses; address fear-of-redundancy with reskilling commitments. |
| **K — Knowledge** | Knowing *how* to operate the new system | Role-based training: 16 hours for end-users, 40 hours for super-users, with sandbox environments. |
| **A — Ability** | Demonstrated competence in real work | 4-week shadowing period post-training; floor-walking support; "super-user buddy" system. |
| **R — Reinforcement** | Sustaining the new behavior | Adoption dashboards reviewed monthly; top adopters recognized in town halls; system usage tied to KPIs. |

*(Award 1.4 marks for each well-explained element with a concrete ERP intervention.)*

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** What is **Change Management**? Differentiate between the **3-Stage Lewin Model** and the **8-Step Kotter Model**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **types of resistance** employees typically exhibit during an ERP roll-out. Suggest a comprehensive **resistance management strategy** with at least five concrete actions. *(7 marks)*

---

**Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Definition + Lewin vs. Kotter (7 marks)**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Change Management is the structured process of preparing, equipping, and supporting individuals to successfully adopt and sustain new ERP-driven business processes, technology, and organizational structures. It focuses on the *people side* of transformation.

**Comparison Table (5 marks — distribute marks across rows and the conclusion):**

| Parameter | Lewin's 3-Stage Model | Kotter's 8-Step Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Kurt Lewin, 1947 (psychologist) | John Kotter, Harvard, 1995 |
| **Number of Stages** | 3 (Unfreeze, Change, Refreeze) | 8 sequential steps |
| **Granularity** | High-level, conceptual | Detailed, action-oriented |
| **Focus** | Psychological force-field analysis | Sequential leadership-driven roadmap |
| **Strength** | Simplicity, foundational theory | Practical, prescriptive, momentum-building |
| **Limitation** | Too abstract for execution | Risk of skipping steps; assumes linear flow |
| **ERP Fit** | Useful for the *macro phases* of ERP roll-out | Useful for the *detailed week-by-week program plan* |

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** In ERP practice, the two are often used together — Lewin defines the *phases*, while Kotter populates each phase with *concrete steps*.

---

**(b) Types of Resistance + Management Strategy (7 marks)**

**Types of Resistance (3 marks — 1 mark each for 3 valid types):**
1. **Active Resistance** — open opposition, e.g., public criticism of the ERP in meetings.
2. **Passive Resistance** — silent non-compliance, e.g., continuing to maintain parallel paper records.
3. **Deferred Resistance** — initial cooperation followed by regression months after go-live.

*(Also accept: Individual vs. Group resistance; Rational vs. Emotional resistance.)*

**Resistance Management Strategy (4 marks — 0.8 marks per concrete action, 5 actions):**
1. **Active Executive Sponsorship** — the CFO personally communicates the vision and is visibly seen using the new ERP system.
2. **Early Involvement and Co-Creation** — invite resistant employees to be process designers; ownership reduces opposition.
3. **Comprehensive Role-Based Training** — hands-on practice in a sandbox with realistic data builds confidence.
4. **Change Champion Network** — train respected peers to act as coaches and feedback gatherers on the shop floor.
5. **Incentive Alignment** — modify performance KPIs to reward correct and timely ERP usage.
6. **Rapid Issue Resolution** — establish a 24×7 helpdesk and feedback channel during the first 90 days post-go-live.
7. **Celebrate and Communicate Wins** — public recognition of early adopters creates positive peer pressure.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 1. **Conflating Training with Change Management.** Training is only the *Knowledge* part of ADKAR. Writing "we will train them" as the entire CM plan loses at least 4 marks in a 14-mark question.
> 2. **Forgetting the Executive Sponsor.** A CM plan that does not name a C-level sponsor and describe their visible role is considered incomplete.
> 3. **Skipping the Sustainment / Refreeze phase.** Many students write elaborate plans for go-live and stop. KTU examiners specifically allocate marks for *post-go-live reinforcement* and KPI tracking.
> 4. **Listing ADKAR or Kotter steps without ERP-specific examples.** Generic management text copied from any textbook will not earn full marks — every step must be tied to an ERP scenario (Finance, HR, Production module, etc.).
> 5. **Ignoring the 70% failure statistic.** When justifying "why CM is critical," failing to quote the well-known *70% ERP failure due to people-side issues* statistic costs easy marks.
> 6. **Confusing Prosci (ADKAR) with Lewin/Kotter in definitions.** ADKAR is a *people-centric* model; Lewin and Kotter are *organization-centric*. Examiners test this distinction deliberately.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition to Memorize:** Change Management is the *structured approach to transitioning people, processes, and culture* during ERP adoption — it is **not** the software installation.
- **The 70% Statistic:** ~70% of ERP failures are due to *people-side* issues, not technical defects. Use this in any "justify the importance of CM" question.
- **The 80/20 Rule:** ~20% of ERP work is technical, ~80% is change-related.
- **Three Models to Master:**
  - **Lewin:** Unfreeze → Change → Refreeze (the simplest).
  - **Kotter:** 8 steps, action-oriented, sequential.
  - **ADKAR:** Awareness → Desire → Knowledge → Ability → Reinforcement (individual-focused, measurable).
- **Roles to Name:** **Executive Sponsor** (C-level, removes roadblocks) + **Change Champion Network** (peer influencers) + **Change Manager** (operational owner) + **Super-Users** (subject-matter experts and trainers).
- **F.L.O.W.E.R.S. Mnemonic for Resistance:** Fear, Loss of control, Old habits, Workload, Ego, Rumors, Skepticism.
- **ADKAR's Hardest Element:** **Desire** — you cannot train or reward your way into desire; it requires trust, sponsorship, and personal engagement.
- **Sustainment (Refreeze) Tools:** Updated KPIs, performance reviews tied to system use, SOPs, recognition programs, adoption dashboards.
- **Adoption KPIs to Quote in Answers:**
  - Adoption Rate (target ≥ 90%)
  - Data Quality Index (target ≥ 95%)
  - Training Completion Rate (target = 100%)
  - Helpdesk Ticket Volume (a falling trend = success).
- **PDCA Loop:** Plan → Do → Check → Act is the underlying sustainment rhythm — mention it whenever discussing post-go-live activities.
- **Common KTU High-Yield Phrases to Use in Answers:**
  - "People-side of change"
  - "As-is vs. to-be state"
  - "Visible executive sponsorship"
  - "Anchor in culture"
  - "Sustained adoption, not just go-live"
- **Link to Other Modules:** Change Management outcomes feed directly into **Module 3 (Post-Implementation)** — keep your answers forward-compatible.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application Support
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET635 - ENTERPRISE RESOURCE PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Application Support

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU's 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PEIET635 – Enterprise Resource Planning**, *Application Support* refers to the **post-implementation lifecycle phase of an ERP system** wherein trained functional and technical personnel ensure the **continuous, uninterrupted, and optimized operation** of the deployed software across the enterprise. It encompasses **incident management, problem resolution, change management, release/patch deployment, user training, performance tuning, and Service Level Agreement (SLA) compliance** for business-critical modules such as Finance, HR, Supply Chain, and Manufacturing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Application Support is the *bridge between Go-Live and Business Value Realization*. Without structured support, even the most successful ERP implementation loses 40–60% of its projected ROI within 18 months.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Emergency Ward"

Imagine you have just built a **super-specialty hospital** (this is your ERP system). Once it opens, patients (business users) start arriving with various ailments (incidents, bugs, queries, training needs). The **Application Support team is essentially the hospital's Emergency + Outpatient + Diagnostics wing combined**:

- **Reception Desk** → *Service Desk / Helpdesk* (logs every user issue).
- **Triage Nurse** → *Tier-1 Support* (filters and resolves basic issues like password resets).
- **Specialist Doctor** → *Tier-2 Functional Consultant* (resolves module-specific functional errors).
- **Surgeon / Super-Specialist** → *Tier-3 Technical / ABAP Consultant* (handles core code, performance, integrations).
- **Medical Records Department** → *Knowledge Base / CMDB* (stores historical solutions).
- **Hospital Director** → *Support Manager / IT Governance* (monitors SLAs, budgets, vendor relationships).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Principle:** Application Support is **reactive + proactive**. Reactive = fixing what breaks. Proactive = monitoring, patching, and optimizing *before* things break.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Table (KTU Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition | KTU Relevance |
|------|------------|---------------|
| **Incident** | Any unplanned interruption to an ERP service | Tracked in ticketing tool (e.g., ServiceNow, JIRA) |
| **Problem** | Root cause of one or more incidents | Managed via Problem Management process |
| **SLA** | Service Level Agreement — contractual commitment on response/resolution time | Often a **14-mark** exam topic |
| **CMDB** | Configuration Management Database | Stores CI (Configuration Items) of the ERP landscape |
| **Change Request (CR)** | Formal proposal to modify the ERP system | Routed through CAB (Change Advisory Board) |
| **Hyper-care** | Intensive post-Go-Live support (typically 4–8 weeks) | High-yield 3-mark question |
| **AMS** | Application Management Services — outsourced support model | Industry buzzword |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of ERP Application Support

### Pillar 1 — Incident & Problem Management (ITIL-Aligned)

The **ITIL (Information Technology Infrastructure Library) v4 framework** is the global gold standard adopted by KTU for describing support processes. The flow is:

1. **Incident Logging** → User reports issue via Service Desk (call, email, portal).
2. **Categorization & Prioritization** → Based on **Impact × Urgency** matrix.
3. **Tier-1 Triage** → Resolve if known (password, basic config) within **15 minutes**.
4. **Escalation to Tier-2** → Functional consultant handles module-specific errors.
5. **Escalation to Tier-3** → Technical/ABAP developer handles code-level or integration faults.
6. **Resolution & Closure** → User confirms fix; ticket closed.
7. **Problem Management** → If same incident repeats, RCA (Root Cause Analysis) is performed and a **Known Error Database (KEDB)** entry is created.

### Pillar 2 — Change & Release Management

Every modification — even a minor report tweak — must follow:

1. **Raise Change Request (RFC)** → Business justification, risk, rollback plan.
2. **CAB Review** → Change Advisory Board evaluates impact.
3. **Test in Quality (DEV → QA)** → Regression testing mandatory.
4. **Transport to Production** → During approved maintenance window.
5. **Post-Implementation Review (PIR)**.

### Pillar 3 — Service Level Management & Continuous Improvement

- **Monitoring** via SAP Solution Manager / Oracle OEM / custom dashboards.
- **Monthly SLA reports** to steering committee.
- **CSI (Continual Service Improvement)** via PDCA cycle (Plan-Do-Check-Act).

## 2.2 Support Models — In-house vs. Outsourced

| Model | Description | Pros | Cons |
|-------|-------------|------|------|
| **In-house AMS** | Company's own IT team supports ERP | Full control, deep domain knowledge | High fixed cost, skill gaps |
| **Vendor AMS** | ERP vendor (SAP/Oracle) provides support | Certified experts, latest patches | Expensive, vendor lock-in |
| **Partner / Third-party AMS** | Certified partner like Infosys, TCS, Wipro | Cost-effective, flexible | Knowledge transfer risk |
| **Hybrid (Co-managed)** | Mix of in-house + partner | Balanced cost & control | Coordination overhead |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Support Metrics & KPIs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield for KTU Numerical Questions:** Memorize these formulas. They appear regularly as 7-mark sub-parts in Part B.

$$
\text{SLA Compliance \%} = \frac{\text{Number of incidents resolved within SLA}}{\text{Total number of incidents}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{First Call Resolution (FCR \%)} = \frac{\text{Incidents resolved at Tier-1}}{\text{Total incidents logged}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{MTTR (Mean Time To Resolve)} = \frac{\sum \text{Resolution time of all incidents}}{\text{Number of incidents}}
$$

$$
\text{MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)} = \frac{\text{Total operational time}}{\text{Number of failures}}
$$

$$
\text{Availability \%} = \frac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Uptime + Downtime}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Total Cost of Support (TCS)} = \text{FTE Cost} + \text{License Cost} + \text{Infrastructure Cost} + \text{Training Cost}
$$

$$
\text{Support ROI \%} = \frac{\text{Benefits Gained from Support (Downtime prevented + Productivity gain)}}{\text{Total Support Cost}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Defect Density} = \frac{\text{Number of defects identified}}{\text{Size of module (Function Points or KLOC)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Avoidance Tip:** Never use the vertical pipe `|` symbol inside markdown tables for absolute value. In LaTeX, write $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ to preserve table formatting.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing Plants:** A 1-hour ERP downtime can halt an assembly line causing losses of **₹5–50 lakhs/hour**.
- **Retail Chains:** POS integration failure during festival season → revenue dip of **10–15%**.
- **Banking:** Core banking ERP downtime is governed by **RBI regulations** (max 4 hours/month).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked-Out Numerical Example — SLA Compliance Calculation

> **KTU-Style Problem:** A manufacturing unit using SAP ERP logged the following incident data for the month of June:
> - Total incidents logged: **240**
> - Incidents resolved within SLA: **198**
> - Incidents escalated to Tier-2: **120**
> - Tier-2 resolution average time: **4.5 hours**
> - Total operational hours in June: **720 hours**
> - Total downtime across all incidents: **12 hours**
> - Estimated productivity loss prevented by support team: **₹18,00,000**
> - Total support cost for the month: **₹6,00,000**
>
> **Calculate:** (a) SLA Compliance %, (b) FCR %, (c) Availability %, (d) Support ROI %.

### Part (a) — SLA Compliance %

We substitute the values into the standard formula:

$$
\text{SLA Compliance \%} = \frac{\text{Incidents resolved within SLA}}{\text{Total incidents}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{SLA Compliance \%} = \frac{198}{240} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{SLA Compliance \%} = 0.825 \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{SLA Compliance \%} = 82.5\%}
$$

### Part (b) — First Call Resolution (FCR) %

Here, incidents *resolved at Tier-1* = Total incidents − Incidents escalated to Tier-2.

$$
\text{Incidents at Tier-1} = 240 - 120 = 120
$$

$$
\text{FCR \%} = \frac{120}{240} \times 100 = 50\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{FCR \%} = 50\%}
$$

### Part (c) — Availability %

$$
\text{Availability \%} = \frac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Uptime + Downtime}} \times 100
$$

Uptime = Total operational time − Downtime = $720 - 12 = 708$ hours.

$$
\text{Availability \%} = \frac{708}{720} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Availability \%} = 0.9833 \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Availability \%} = 98.33\%}
$$

### Part (d) — Support ROI %

$$
\text{Support ROI \%} = \frac{\text{Benefits gained}}{\text{Total support cost}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Support ROI \%} = \frac{18{,}00{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Support ROI \%} = 3 \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Support ROI \%} = 300\%}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Every ₹1 spent on Application Support generates ₹3 in business value — an excellent ROI justifying continued investment.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Severity vs. Response Time (Industry Standard)

| Severity | Business Impact | Response Time (SLA) | Resolution Time | Escalation Path |
|----------|-----------------|---------------------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Sev-1 (Critical)** | Entire ERP down / revenue loss | ≤ **15 minutes** | ≤ **4 hours** | L1 → L2 → L3 → Vendor in 30 min |
| **Sev-2 (High)** | Single module down, workaround exists | ≤ **1 hour** | ≤ **8 hours** | L1 → L2 → L3 |
| **Sev-3 (Medium)** | Minor issue, low impact | ≤ **4 hours** | ≤ **3 business days** | L1 → L2 |
| **Sev-4 (Low)** | Cosmetic / enhancement request | ≤ **1 business day** | Best-effort | L1 only |

## 3.3 Hyper-care Phase — Analytical Breakdown

| Week | Activity | Team Strength | Focus |
|------|----------|---------------|-------|
| Week 1–2 | Stabilization | **100%** (full team on-site) | Critical defects, data issues |
| Week 3–4 | Knowledge transfer | **75%** | Documenting resolutions |
| Week 5–6 | Steady-state transition | **50%** | Training internal team |
| Week 7–8 | Handover to AMS | **25%** | Final sign-off |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick Question Alert:** "Hyper-care ends when…" — The correct answer is *when incident volume stabilizes and SLA compliance > 95%*, NOT after a fixed number of weeks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ERP Application Support — Multi-Tier Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[End User Reports Issue] --> B[Service Desk Portal]
    B --> C{Tier-1 Triage}
    C -->|Known Issue| D[Resolve and Close]
    C -->|Complex Issue| E[Tier-2 Functional Consultant]
    E -->|Module-level Fix| F[Resolve and Document]
    E -->|Code-level Issue| G[Tier-3 Technical Consultant]
    G -->|ABAP / Integration Fix| H[Quality Testing]
    H --> I[Transport to Production]
    I --> J[User Confirmation and Closure]
    D --> K[KEDB Update]
    F --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Monthly SLA Report]
    L --> M[Steering Committee Review]
    M --> N[CSI Improvements]
```

## 4.2 Incident Management Lifecycle (ITIL v4 Aligned)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Incident Detected] --> P2[Log Incident]
    P2 --> P3[Categorize]
    P3 --> P4[Prioritize]
    P4 --> P5[Diagnose]
    P5 --> P6[Escalate if needed]
    P6 --> P7[Resolve]
    P7 --> P8[Close Incident]
    P8 --> P9{RCA needed?}
    P9 -->|Yes| P10[Problem Ticket]
    P9 -->|No| P11[Archive]
    P10 --> P12[KEDB]
    P12 --> P11
```

## 4.3 Support Model Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Choose Support Model] --> Q1{Budget Constraint?}
    Q1 -->|Tight| Q2[Third-Party AMS]
    Q1 -->|Flexible| Q3{Domain Criticality?}
    Q3 -->|High| Q4[Vendor AMS]
    Q3 -->|Moderate| Q5[Hybrid Co-Managed]
    Q5 --> Q6[In-house AMS]
    Q2 --> End[Final Selection]
    Q4 --> End
    Q6 --> End
```

## 4.4 Change Management Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CR1[Raise Change Request] --> CR2[Impact Analysis]
    CR2 --> CR3{CAB Review}
    CR3 -->|Approved| CR4[Schedule Change]
    CR3 -->|Rejected| CR5[Inform Requester]
    CR4 --> CR6[Dev Environment Build]
    CR6 --> CR7[QA Testing]
    CR7 --> CR8{Tests Passed?}
    CR8 -->|Yes| CR9[Production Deploy]
    CR8 -->|No| CR10[Rework]
    CR10 --> CR6
    CR9 --> CR11[PIR and Closure]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Application Support in the context of ERP. List any four key activities performed by the Application Support team.**

**Model Answer:**
Application Support refers to the post-implementation phase of an ERP system that ensures continuous, uninterrupted functioning of the deployed software through monitoring, issue resolution, and continuous improvement.

**Four key activities:**
1. Incident logging and resolution via Service Desk.
2. Change and release management for system modifications.
3. User training and knowledge base creation.
4. SLA monitoring and performance optimization.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for activities with brief explanation)*

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is Hyper-care in ERP implementation? Why is it critical?**

**Model Answer:**
Hyper-care is the **intensive post-Go-Live support period (typically 4–8 weeks)** where the project team is deployed on-site to resolve critical defects, stabilize the system, and train end-users.

**Why critical:**
- Immediate resolution of high-severity incidents.
- Builds user confidence in the new system.
- Captures lessons learned for steady-state operations.

*(3 marks — 1.5 for definition, 1.5 for criticality)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Pattern]

**(a) Explain the three-tier support model of an ERP system with a neat diagram. Describe the roles and responsibilities of each tier.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The **three-tier support model** is the industry-standard structure for ERP Application Support, ensuring efficient escalation and resolution of issues.

**Tier-1 — Service Desk / First-Level Support**
- Acts as the **Single Point of Contact (SPOC)** for end-users.
- Handles **basic, repetitive issues** such as password resets, login problems, and navigation queries.
- Logs all incidents in the ticketing tool.
- Target: resolve **50–60% of incidents at this level (FCR)**.
- Response SLA: ≤ **15 minutes** for Sev-1.

**Tier-2 — Functional / Module Support**
- Handled by **functional consultants** (e.g., FI/CO, MM, SD, PP, HR).
- Resolves **module-specific functional errors** such as incorrect tax calculation, purchase order workflow issues, payroll miscalculations.
- Performs **configuration changes** and master data corrections.
- Escalates technical/code-level issues to Tier-3.
- Response SLA: ≤ **1–4 hours** depending on severity.

**Tier-3 — Technical / Development Support**
- Handled by **ABAP / Java developers, basis administrators, integration specialists**.
- Resolves **code-level bugs, performance issues, RFC failures, IDoc errors**.
- Manages **transport requests, patches, kernel upgrades**.
- Coordinates with **ERP vendor (SAP/Oracle)** for product-level defects.
- Response SLA: ≤ **4–8 hours** for Sev-1.

**Advantages:**
- Reduces resolution time via specialization.
- Improves user satisfaction.
- Enables cost optimization (cheaper Tier-1 staff handle bulk of tickets).

*(7 marks — 2 for diagram explanation, 5 for tier-wise roles)*

**(b) A pharmaceutical company using ERP logged 500 incidents in Q1. 280 were resolved at Tier-1, 150 at Tier-2, and 70 at Tier-3. Calculate FCR %, Tier-2 Escalation %, and the support cost if average cost per incident at L1 = ₹500, L2 = ₹2500, L3 = ₹8000.** *(7 marks)*

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate FCR %**

$$
\text{FCR \%} = \frac{\text{Tier-1 resolved}}{\text{Total}} \times 100 = \frac{280}{500} \times 100 = 56\%
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Tier-2 Escalation %**

$$
\text{Tier-2 Escalation \%} = \frac{280 + 150}{500} \times 100 = \frac{430}{500} \times 100 = 86\%
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Total Support Cost**

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{L1}} = 280 \times 500 = 1{,}40{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{L2}} = 150 \times 2500 = 3{,}75{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{L3}} = 70 \times 8000 = 5{,}60{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Total Cost} = 1{,}40{,}000 + 3{,}75{,}000 + 5{,}60{,}000 = 10{,}75{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Total Support Cost} = \text{₹}10{,}75{,}000}
$$

*(7 marks — 2 for FCR, 2 for escalation %, 3 for cost calculation)*

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Pattern]

**(a) Discuss the Incident Management process in ERP support as per ITIL framework. Include a flowchart in your explanation.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The **ITIL v4 Incident Management** process aims to **restore normal service operation as quickly as possible** and minimize adverse impact on business operations.

**Process Steps:**

1. **Incident Detection and Logging** — User reports issue via Service Desk; ticket is created with unique ID, timestamp, and category.
2. **Categorization** — Incident is classified by module (FI, MM, SD, HR, PP) and type (functional, technical, data, integration).
3. **Prioritization** — Based on **Impact × Urgency matrix** into Sev-1 to Sev-4.
4. **Initial Diagnosis (Tier-1)** — Service desk agent attempts resolution using knowledge base.
5. **Escalation** — If unresolved, escalated to Tier-2 (functional) or Tier-3 (technical) with full ticket context.
6. **Investigation and Diagnosis** — Functional/technical consultant performs root cause analysis.
7. **Resolution and Recovery** — Fix is applied; for Sev-1, workaround is provided immediately, permanent fix later.
8. **Incident Closure** — User confirms resolution; ticket is closed with resolution notes.
9. **Post-Incident Review** — For Sev-1, RCA is performed; lessons added to KEDB.

**Key Performance Indicators:**
- Mean Time to Acknowledge (MTTA)
- Mean Time to Resolve (MTTR)
- SLA Compliance %
- First Call Resolution %

*(7 marks — 1 for flowchart, 4 for step explanation, 2 for KPIs)*

**(b) Compare In-house AMS, Vendor AMS, and Third-party AMS support models. Recommend the best model for a mid-sized manufacturing company with 800 employees and an annual IT budget of ₹2 crore.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Criteria | In-house AMS | Vendor AMS | Third-party AMS |
|----------|--------------|------------|------------------|
| **Cost** | High (₹1–1.5 Cr/year for 5 FTE) | Very High (₹1.5–2 Cr/year) | Moderate (₹60L–80L/year) |
| **Expertise** | Limited to team's exposure | Highest (certified) | Good (depends on partner) |
| **Control** | Full | Low | Moderate |
| **Scalability** | Difficult | Easy | Easy |
| **Knowledge Retention** | High | Low | Moderate |
| **Risk** | Skill gaps | Vendor lock-in | Knowledge transfer risk |

**Recommendation — Hybrid (Co-managed) Model:**

For the given mid-sized manufacturing firm:
- **In-house team of 2–3 functional consultants** for Tier-1 and Tier-2 (handles daily queries, master data, training).
- **Third-party partner for Tier-3** (technical, ABAP, integrations, performance tuning).
- **Vendor (SAP/Oracle) for product-level issues** via annual support contract (~15–20% of license cost).

**Justification:**
- Budget constraint (₹2 Cr) cannot support full vendor AMS.
- In-house ensures domain knowledge and control over critical processes.
- Partner reduces technical skill gap cost-effectively.
- Estimated annual cost: **₹85–95 lakhs**, well within budget.

*(7 marks — 3 for comparison table, 4 for recommendation with justification)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - ❌ **Do NOT** write only definitions in 14-mark questions. You **MUST** include diagrams, tables, and numerical solutions.
> - ❌ **Do NOT** skip steps in numerical sub-parts. Marks are awarded for *each step*. A correct final answer with no working = **50% marks only**.
> - ❌ **Do NOT** confuse *Incident Management* with *Problem Management*. Incidents restore service; Problems find root cause.
> - ✅ **Always** cite ITIL v4 framework wherever applicable — examiners reward process-oriented answers.
> - ✅ **Always** include a labelled diagram in 7-mark theory questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Application Support** is the *post-Go-Live* phase ensuring ERP delivers business value continuously.
- **Three-tier support model**: Tier-1 (Service Desk) → Tier-2 (Functional) → Tier-3 (Technical).
- **ITIL v4 framework** governs incident, problem, and change management.
- **Hyper-care** = 4–8 weeks intensive post-Go-Live support.
- **Key SLAs**: Sev-1 ≤ 15 min response / 4 hr resolution; Sev-4 ≤ 1 day.
- **Critical KPIs**: SLA Compliance %, FCR %, MTTR, MTBF, Availability %, Support ROI.
- **Support Models**: In-house | Vendor | Third-party | **Hybrid (most common)**.
- **Change Management** must follow CAB approval — no ad-hoc changes.
- **KEDB (Known Error Database)** is the institutional memory of the support team.
- **CSI (Continual Service Improvement)** via PDCA cycle keeps support evolving.
- **Numericals to master**: SLA %, FCR %, Availability %, MTTR, Total Cost, Support ROI.
- **Always draw a diagram** in 7-mark answers — flowchart of incident lifecycle is most popular.
- **AMSP (Application Management Service Provider)** contracts are typically 3–5 years.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Human Capital Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET635 - ENTERPRISE RESOURCE PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Human Capital Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Human Capital Management (HCM)** is a comprehensive, strategic, and technology-driven framework within Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) systems that focuses on the acquisition, development, optimization, and retention of an organization's workforce. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme curriculum, HCM is defined as the integrated set of processes, practices, and systems used to manage the human resources of an enterprise, aligning workforce capabilities with organizational objectives through a unified software suite.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Human Capital Management (HCM) is the ERP sub-system that treats employees as *assets* (capital) rather than costs, integrating all HR functions—from recruitment to retirement—into a single data-driven platform to maximize organizational performance.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Think of HCM as the **"command center for a sports team"**. Just as a football team has:

- A **scout** (Recruitment) who finds the best new players
- A **coach** (Training & Development) who improves their skills
- A **physiotherapist** (Time & Attendance, Leave) who tracks fitness
- A **salary manager** (Payroll & Compensation) who handles contracts
- A **performance analyst** (Performance Management) who tracks goals
- A **club president** (Strategic HR Planning) who decides long-term direction

HCM consolidates all these roles into one integrated software system so that data flows seamlessly. If the scout signs a new player, the salary manager, coach, and physiotherapist are *automatically* informed through a single database.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** HCM is the *modern, technology-enabled evolution* of traditional **Human Resource Management (HRM)**. While HRM is administrative and paper-based, HCM is strategic, data-driven, and cloud-enabled.

## 1.3 Standard Terminology Used in HCM

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **HCM** | Human Capital Management |
| **HRIS** | Human Resource Information System |
| **HRMS** | Human Resource Management System |
| **Talent Management** | End-to-end employee growth lifecycle |
| **Self-Service Portal** | Employee/Manager interface for HR transactions |
| **Core HR** | Central database of employee master data |
| **Payroll** | Salary, tax, and benefits processing |
| **TNA** | Training Needs Analysis |
| **KPI** | Key Performance Indicator (used in appraisals) |

## 1.4 Position of HCM in ERP Architecture

HCM is one of the largest functional modules in any ERP suite (SAP, Oracle, Microsoft Dynamics, Tally, etc.). It typically accounts for **30–40%** of total ERP implementation cost and time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The KTU syllabus positions HCM as the *strategic HR* layer that converts traditional administrative HR functions into real-time, analytical, and employee-centric operations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
